text: The Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, Volume Twelve of the Miscellaneous Section *Records of Old Stories of the Gentry of Luoyang* *First Category of the Novelists' Works [Miscellaneous Events]* **Summary** By the officials, we respectfully note that *Records of Old Stories of the Gentry of Luoyang* consists of five volumes, compiled by Zhang Qixian of the Song dynasty. Zhang Qixian, courtesy name Shiliang, was from Caozhou but later moved to Luoyang. He passed the imperial examinations in the second year of Taiping Xingguo and held various high-ranking positions, including Vice-Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, before retiring as a Sikong. He died posthumously with the title Wending. His biography is recorded in the *Song History*. The preface of this book is dated to the second year of Jingde, during the reign of Emperor Zhenzong, when Zhang Qixian served as the governor of Qingzhou as the Minister of War. The book recounts old stories of Luoyang from the Later Liang and Later Tang dynasties onward, comprising twenty-one chapters divided into five volumes. The catalog and index of the book align with this version, though Chao Cuo's *Record of Books Read* lists it as ten volumes. However, Zhang's preface explicitly states that the book consists of five volumes, and the *Yongle Dadian* also confirms that no additional content exists beyond this version. Thus, the error in Chao's catalog is clear. The book primarily draws from legends and folktales, recording facts in a concise manner to serve as moral admonitions. Zhang claims that any discrepancies from the official histories are included as supplementary or alternative records, akin to *biographies* or *external biographies*. However, certain entries, such as the story of Zhou Ling's wife in Hengyang, the dyer in Luoyang who encounters a wronged ghost, and Jiao Sheng who sees his deceased wife, inevitably involve supernatural elements.

### Translation Discussing strange events, such as the story of the virtuous wife of Li Shao, mentions that the Khitan prince Dongdan, who had fled to the Tang, was secretly killed by the Later Tang court and died an unnatural death. The Khitan already knew of this, and Li Su was ordered to guard the funeral casket and send it back, feeling distressed and unable to think of a plan. According to the "Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government" and the "History of the Five Dynasties," Dongdan was the eldest son of Emperor Taizu of Liao and the elder brother of Emperor Taizong of Liao. He fled to the Tang and was granted the title of Zan Hua. Later, due to Emperor Taizong's assistance in the uprising of Shi Jin, the Prince of Lu sent the eunuch Qin Jimin and the military official Li Yanshen to kill him in his residence. Thus, Dongdan's death was actually due to the Prince of Lu's anger over his military defeat, which quickly led to the downfall of the Later Tang. After gaining the throne, Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin prepared a proper funeral ceremony and sent him back. The event occurred across two dynasties, and in the Tang, there was no secret plot to harm him. Why should there be any suspicion that would immediately cause distress? This matter is likely based on erroneous hearsay and is not credible. As for recording Zhang Quanyi's achievements in governing Luoyang, the details are extremely detailed, and the old histories often adopted these accounts. Other lesser-known events are also worth reading for broadening one's knowledge. They can be read alongside the missing sections of the "History of the Five Dynasties" for historical verification. Submitted in the third month of the forty-sixth year of the Qianlong era.

Chief Editor [Subject] Ji Yun, [Subject] Lu Xixiong, [Subject] Sun Shiyi Chief Proofreader [Subject] Lu Feichi Preface to the Records of Old News in Luoyang For several years before I took the imperial examinations, I spent much time with elderly scholars from the Luoyang gentry class, who told me stories about the Tang and Later Liang periods. These stories often praised or criticized past events with clear and logical reasoning, making them enjoyable to listen to for an entire day. However, I soon forgot them after writing them down. For decades, I had no time to write, and now, due to old age, I have forgotten much. Recently, matters in Yingqiu have occupied my time, and I have been sitting idly all day, without the energy or focus to write. Reflecting on the stories told by the gentry and my own personal experiences, I have compiled over twenty stories into five volumes. I have included stories based on facts, following the examples of previous historians, and have omitted trivial disputes. Stories that differ from the official histories are also included, and I have called the work "Records of Old News in Luoyang," hoping that it will be a reliable source of information. This work is based on the discussions of the gentry and is intended to be a trustworthy record. I hope that readers will find it enlightening. Preface written in the summer of the year 1075 in Yingqiu by Zhang Qixian.

The Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries Volume 1 of the Records of Old News of the Officials and Scholars of Luoyang Compiled by Zhang Qixian of the Song Dynasty The Favorable Treatment of Literati by Emperor Taizu of Later Liang When Emperor Taizu of Later Liang first held control over four commanderies, he was known for his fierce and resolute demeanor, much like a tiger nursing its cubs. Any slight disobedience would result in immediate execution. The officials of the Later Liang court would bid farewell to their families each morning before entering the palace, and upon returning, they would greet each other with congratulations, likely to avoid suspicion. Guests in his presence would tremble with fear. The scholar Du Xunhe submitted his work to Emperor Taizu and requested an audience. The official in charge of guests reported the matter to Emperor Taizu, who remained silent and did not respond. Du Xunhe stayed in Daliang for several months. Previously, anyone who had been introduced to Emperor Taizu but had not yet met him would often stay in inns for years, suffering from cold and hunger. The officials in charge would not allow them to leave privately; otherwise, they would face severe consequences. Du Xunhe visited the guest quarters daily. One day, Emperor Taizu, while in his private quarters, asked where Du Xunhe was. The officials replied that Du Xunhe was in the guest quarters. Soon after, a messenger arrived on horseback. Emperor Taizu saw the messenger and did not leave until noon. He suddenly got up and returned home. Du Xunhe told the official in charge that he was very hungry and wanted to return home. The officials provided him with food and said that if Emperor Taizu came out to see the scholar, they would all be in danger of death.

不暇至申未间梁祖果出复坐于便防令取骰子来既至梁祖掷意似有所卜掷且久终不惬旨怒甚屡顾左右左右怖惧缩颈重足蹈汤火须臾梁祖取骰子在手大呼【去声】曰杜荀鹤掷之六只俱赤乃连声命邀秀才荀鹤来主客者引入令趋骤至阶陛下梁祖言曰秀才不合趋阶荀鹤声喏恐惧流汗再拜叙谢讫命坐荀鹤惨悴战栗神不主体梁祖徐曰知秀才乆矣荀鹤欲降陛拜谢梁祖曰不可于是再拜复坐梁祖顾视陛下谓左右曰似有雨防下令视之实雨也然仰首视之天无片云雨防甚大霑陛檐有声梁祖自起熟视之复坐谓杜曰秀才曽见无云雨否荀鹤答言未曾见梁祖笑 There was no time to reach the period between the hours of Shen and Wei, and Emperor Liang Zhu indeed came out and sat again in the pavilion. He ordered to bring dice, and when they arrived, he threw them, as if intending to divine something. The throwing lasted a long time without satisfying his intention. He became very angry and repeatedly looked around at his attendants, who were terrified, shrinking their necks and stepping heavily as if walking on hot coals. After a moment, Emperor Liang Zhu took the dice in his hand and shouted loudly, "Du Xunhe, throw them!" All six dice came up red, and he called out repeatedly to invite the scholar Du Xunhe. The host introduced him, and he was hurriedly brought to the steps. Emperor Liang Zhu said, "Scholar, you should not rush up the steps." Du Xunhe responded, "Yes," and was filled with fear, sweating profusely. He bowed twice and thanked him, after which he was ordered to sit. Du Xunhe looked pale and trembling, his spirit not steady. Emperor Liang Zhu spoke slowly, "I have known the scholar for a long time." Du Xunhe wanted to prostrate himself and thank him, but Emperor Liang Zhu said, "No." Du Xunhe then bowed twice and sat again. Emperor Liang Zhu looked at the steps and said to his attendants, "It seems there is a rain shelter here." He ordered to look, and it was indeed raining. However, when he looked up, there was no cloud in the sky, but the rain shelter was heavily drenched and made a sound. Emperor Liang Zhu got up and looked closely, then sat down again and said to Du, "Scholar, have you ever seen rain without clouds?" Du Xunhe answered, "I have never seen such a thing." Emperor Liang Zhu laughed.

Liang Zong said, "This is what is called rain without clouds, called 'heaven weeping,' and it is unknown what auspicious sign it represents." He laughed loudly and ordered the attendants to bring paper and ink, asking Du the scholar to compose a poem about rain without clouds. Du, feeling as if sitting on burning charcoal, was extremely anxious and fearful. Liang Zong ordered him again to compose a poem about rain without clouds. Du dared not refuse and immediately composed a poem on the spot. Du quickly composed a quatrain and presented it. Liang Zong read it and was greatly pleased, immediately summoned the guests to drink together, and they parted in great joy. He also said, "The next day, I will specially prepare a banquet for Du the scholar." Du bowed and thanked him before leaving. Du's quatrain read: "Both are matters of heaven and earth, Yet the rain threads fly and scatter across the sun's wheel. If they were all alike, how could we show the creative power of the Liang King?" By this, Du gained Liang Zong's favor. After returning home, Du became so frightened that he fell ill, experiencing diarrhea dozens of times. His appearance and demeanor were extremely weak, and he was barely able to get up. The guest attendant took care of him, providing medicine and soup as if tending to a beloved parent. The next morning, when the host and guests arrived, they urged Du to hurry. They said, "The great king wishes to see the scholar, please mount your horse quickly." Du had no choice but to dress and mount his horse. By the time he arrived, he was called urgently five or seven times. Du was exhausted and weak, worried that he was moving too slowly.

Li Keyong stood up and shouted loudly, "Du Mu, I am requesting a memorial to praise the great achievements of the Prince of Liang." Du Mu, forgetting his illness, walked briskly and bowed repeatedly to express his gratitude. From then on, Li Keyong specially set up a guesthouse for him, gave him clothes and money, and treated him with great respect. Xu Yin, a man from Fujian who had failed the imperial examination, presented an essay about the outskirts of Li Keyong's territory. Li Keyong read it and valued it highly, saying, "In ancient times, there was a saying that a word could be rewarded with a thousand pieces of gold. Since military expenses are high, I will reward each word with one bolt of silk." Xu Yin's essay roughly said, "A guest who was unsuccessful and returned home passed through Daliang and met elderly people in the outskirts. He asked about the current and past kings and elders, and they said, 'Let's talk about the present, not the past.' Many of the words were not recorded, and he was then kept in the guesthouse and treated with great respect." Xu Yin was very ill, and Li Keyong sent someone to say, "Even if it were Qin Shi Huang or Han Wu Di, it criticizes your essay for directly discussing the immortality of Xiang Shi and Wang Qiao, which no one has seen. Even if Qin Shi Huang or Han Wu Di did not die, where would they go? He was upset by these deep and poignant lines." Li Keyong, who had the ambition to move the tripod of the state, sought out honest and outspoken scholars. One day, he suddenly said something significant.

Outside the city gates of Liang, dozens of miles away, Li Keyong rested under a tall willow tree. The tree was several times around in circumference, with branches that were very large, capable of providing shelter for fifty or sixty people. Travelers also joined him, and Li Keyong sat alone, speaking to himself, "What a great willow tree!" He then slowly and thoroughly looked at the guests, staring at them for a long time. The seated guests each got up and replied, "A great willow tree!" Li Keyong said again, "This great willow tree is good for making the head of a carriage." A few people sitting at the end got up and replied, "Good for making the head of a carriage." Li Keyong turned to Jing Xiang and others, who replied, "Even if the willow tree is good for making the head of a carriage, it must be paired with elm trees." Li Keyong suddenly spoke in a stern voice, "These scholars love to flatter people with smooth talk. How can a willow tree be good for making the head of a carriage? The head of a carriage must be made with elm trees. I said the willow tree is good for making the head of a carriage. I heard people say during the Qin dynasty that a deer was called a horse—what is difficult about it?" He then said to those around him, "Wait a little while longer." Fifty or seventy warriors caught those who said the willow tree is good for making the head of a carriage and killed them on the spot for flattery. Li Keyong, though he rose from among the bandits, was ruthless and suspicious, more so than ancient rulers. His boldness, decisiveness, and use of strategy to control others led to the establishment of his dynasty, which was not accidental.

The False Madman [Yang Gong, Style Name Ningshi] Yang Gong, the Right Minister Ningshi, is recorded in the official histories as a man of broad learning in classical texts, skilled in both writing and calligraphy. His brushwork was robust and distinctive, forming a unique school of its own. He was broad-minded and generous in character, often keeping himself obscure. Despite his talents, he was not given high office and frequently feigned madness to avoid attention. [At the time, officials secretly referred to him as "Yang the Madman."] In Luoyang, he often visited Buddhist temples and Taoist shrines, and whenever he encountered places of natural beauty—cool, secluded, and picturesque—he would wander freely and feel at ease, singing and reciting poetry and forgetting to return. Consequently, his handwriting was often found on the walls of temples and shrines, which were guarded and treasured. If a monk saw that Yang Gong had not yet left his mark on a particular wall, they would first prepare it by whitewashing and cleaning it, waiting for his arrival. Upon entering the temple, if he saw the wall was clean and appealing, he would sit cross-legged, look around as if mad, and begin to write and recite poetry, his brush moving in harmony with his spirit. He would write until the wall was fully covered, and then stop, showing no sign of fatigue. Visitors who witnessed this were always amazed and praised him. Therefore, the second son of Feng Yingwang, the Junior Secretary, left an inscription on the temple wall saying, "The true works of the Right Minister fill the monks' abode; even Zhong and Wang would be surpassed. Please, Master Yuan, cherish this writing."

There is nothing like the five things in the world: a scholar named An Hongjian, a stone inkstone inscribed with "Cloudy Stream," a stone inkstone managed by the Xuanhe Bureau, Wutai Mountain ink, and purple rabbit hair brush. During the Later Jin period, Zhang Xiang, the chancellor, was granted the title of "South Palace Envoy" and later served as the acting governor of Western Capital (Luoyang). Shortly after arriving in Luoyang, a scholar from Dongjing (Kaifeng) obtained leave to travel to Luoyang and stayed overnight in Zhongmou County. During the late afternoon, a swarm of locusts blocked out the sun, flying from Dongjing. The next morning, they reached Zhengzhou, and by evening, a smaller swarm arrived. The following day, they reached Yingyang, where another swarm appeared. Coincidentally, there was someone traveling to Luoyang by official carriage, and the scholar attached a letter and a poem, which he sent first to the governor of Luoyang, Zhang Gong. The poem read: "Leading the locusts to Luoyang, the governor should welcome them far away." [The scholar then said, "Clouds and clouds."] After arriving in Luoyang, the scholar stayed for several days and then sent a poem to Zhang Xiang, the chancellor of the South Palace, who was serving as the acting governor of Luoyang. The poem praised Zhang's governance: "The South Palace Chancellor governs Luoyang; within three months, his reputation spreads far and wide. All officials follow his example, and even ordinary people see peace. Knowing his poverty, Zhang Gong gave him generous gifts. The scholar's residence is in the government office."

A woman lived near the western gate of the official residence, just a short distance away. She often entered the official residence with a sedan chair in front and a horse behind her, accompanied by an elderly teacher named Shao Shi, who carried a staff, wore a cap, and dressed in a robe. Shao Shi walked a few dozen steps behind her at a leisurely pace, and those who saw them could not understand their behavior, thinking it was a form of feigned madness. As winter approached, the woman lived in a house without proper warm clothing. Shao Shi remained calm and did not inquire about her situation. One day, an old acquaintance returned from a journey with a large amount of luggage. The woman wrote a letter to Shao Shi, complaining about her poverty. The acquaintance arrived early in the morning to visit, leaving the luggage at a store called Tong Li. In the middle of the store, the acquaintance left 50 bolts of silk and 100 bolts of cloth, writing a letter to Shao Shi asking to sell the goods to prepare for winter clothes. Shao Shi received the silk and cloth and sent them all to a temple for Buddhist nuns, where he made socks for several dozen nuns and monks. He also sent all the silk and cloth to the Nan Chan Great Character Two Temples and invited monks to dine at the house. The woman and her family were already showing signs of cold, and an old female servant was surprised and moved to tears upon hearing that the donations were being made to the monks. Shao Shi laughed but did not say anything. After several days, the local governor learned about the situation and called upon the female workers to make woolen clothes for all the people in Yang's household, ensuring no one was lacking. The clothes were made and sent to the household. When Shao Shi saw the clothes being delivered, he smiled and said to the household, "I knew the governor would send clothes."

This is also a case of feigned madness where one's intentions are hard to gauge. Every time he went out, after mounting his horse and reaching the gate, the attendant asked the old servant about the person they were visiting. The old servant said, "Today, it's good to go east to visit Guang'ai Temple." The young master raised his whip and said, "Let's go to Guang'ai Temple," and lashed the horse to head east. The old servant then said, "It's better to go west to visit Shibi Temple." The young master then slowly said, "Let's go to Shibi Temple." Those who heard this secretly laughed. These are all cases of feigned madness. There was a woman named Yang Zulu who was skilled in singing and talking, and she was good at engaging in witty banter and debate, with rare talent and thought, unmatched at the time. The young master called her his niece. He often had her sing, and her words were quick and her tones clear, truly like Qin Qing and Han E. The young master called her his niece because he admired her intelligence and beauty. At that time, there was a monk named Yun Bian who was skilled in secular sermons, had literary talent, and was quick in responding, like a eulogy. He could compose a thousand words on the spot according to the rank and status of the person, all as if pre-prepared. The young master especially valued him. Yun Bian gave a lecture at Changshou Temple in May, and the young master went to the lecture hall.

The scholar and the monk, Yundian, were seated facing each other with a singer beside them. Suddenly, a large spider descended from the eaves by a thread, directly in front of the scholar and the monk. Yundian smiled and said to the singer, "Try to mock this spider. If you can mock it successfully, I will give you two bolts of silk." The singer did not hesitate and immediately began to mock the spider. The words were entirely about the spider but also served as a playful mockery of Yundian. The scholar, upon hearing this, burst out laughing and shouted, "Monk, give him five bolts of silk!" Yundian laughed, and the scholar gave the singer five bolts of silk. The singer's mockery of the spider went like this: "Eat until the belly is full, Crawling down the eaves, Spinning webs in the air, Only waiting to kill living beings." (This was a veiled criticism of Yundian's large and imposing figure.) The monk Yundian, whose name was Yuanjian, later became a high-ranking official and eventually passed away. The scholar had many calligraphy inscriptions on the walls of temples and monasteries in the western capital, but few of these were preserved. After a great flood, the walls collapsed, and most of the inscriptions were lost, which is truly regrettable.

Xiangyang Incident During the reign of Emperor Shizong of Later Jin, Zhang Congen, the Xiangyang Prefect, was serving as the Director of the Southern Office of the Imperial Secretariat. At that time, An Zhongrong, the governor of Chengde, rebelled against the emperor. The day of departure for the imperial expedition was approaching. One night, Zhang thought deeply about the situation. If the emperor were to lead a northern campaign, Prince An Congjin, who was stationed in Xiangyang, already showed signs of rebellion. He feared that the court was unprepared. The next day, after court, he requested an audience with the emperor. He asked for the position of governing Xiangyang and said, "If Prince Congjin were to take advantage of the empty situation to attack the capital, what would Your Majesty do to prepare?" Emperor Shizong replied, "You do not know, but now I have ordered Gao Xingzhou to be the commander-in-chief and appointed you as the supervisor. I have also ordered Gao Xun, Jiao Jixun, and several others to serve as attendants." Upon hearing the emperor's words, Zhang realized that there was already a plan in place, which aligned with his own thoughts. Moreover, since the imperial command had already been issued and the troops were ready, he dared not refuse or decline. He then presented several requests, all of which were approved. First, he sent out cavalry commander Guo Jinhai to lead more than three thousand cavalry to station in Tangzhou. Jiao Jixun and several others also went on this journey. Shortly after Emperor Shizong departed from the capital, Prince An Congjin indeed rebelled, thinking the court had no preparation and wanting to take advantage of the empty situation to suddenly attack. He selected elite cavalry to move south. Jiao Jixun and several others, knowing that Congjin had rebelled, immediately sent a rapid report to the imperial camp. Zhang Congen, Duke of Bohai, also took action.

Subsequently, Congjin (继发従进) met Guo (郭) on Huashan Mountain. Jin Hai (金海), a foreign general skilled in using the spear, rarely engaged in combat. He was exceptionally brave, fond of fighting, and desired to achieve remarkable feats. The two armies were several miles apart. Congjin had always commanded cavalry, and Jin Hai had long been under his command, and he had always treated him well. Congjin then leaped onto his horse, led several hundred cavalry, and advanced to within a hundred steps of Guo's army, shouting loudly, "De Guo, Jin Hai!" Jin Hai rode out alone, advanced several dozen steps, removed his helmet, and stood sideways, shouting loudly, "I am Jin Hai." Congjin then advanced another several dozen steps, greeting him and saying, "Jin Hai, are you well? I have always treated you well, but you show no gratitude today. How dare you come to fight me?" Jin Hai responded loudly, "Your Majesty, what have I done wrong to the Great King? What has the Great King done to me? Today, the Great King has betrayed me. I ask for one arrow's distance to retreat. If you do not allow it, you will face my spear." After speaking, he drew his spear, spurred his horse, and rushed into the array. Gao Xun (高勲) also followed. Congjin was frightened, leaped onto his horse, and retreated. The two armies then engaged, and were heavily defeated by Jin Hai. Jiao Jixun (焦继勲) commanded the rear guard and reported to the Jin Emperor, who was greatly pleased and gave different rewards. From then on, Congjin lost his spirit and fortified the city.

The imperial army fortified the city with multiple layers of defenses and deep trenches, holding it for over a month. Then, the imperial army launched an attack on the city, and arrows rained down from the walls like a storm, wounding many soldiers. On that day, Jinhai was struck by arrows, and he returned to the camp injured. The next day, someone devised a plan to defame Jinhai, aiming to make the court doubt him. They hung a golden bottle filled with wine and a golden box containing medicine on the city wall, calling out to Jinhai. Jinhai, aware of the situation, hurriedly went to the city wall with his wounds, and the person on the wall said, "The great king knows you were hit by arrows and is concerned about your injuries. He has granted you this golden bottle of wine and this box of medicine." Jinhai, a foreigner who was illiterate and only interested in profit, took the bottle and box and returned to the camp without reporting to the commanding general. The commanding generals became suspicious and sent a fast message to the court. Emperor Shizong, remembering Jinhai's contributions to the "Flower Mountain" campaign, did not punish him. Instead, he appointed Jinhai as the commander of the Jinzhou militia and transferred his troops to another unit. Jinhai's position was thus relieved, and he often felt depressed and unhappy, eventually passing away. What a pity. Jiao Jixun, under the reign of Emperor Taizu, was appointed as the governor of Xiangzhou with the authority to command troops. He later served in the Liao dynasty as the governor of Youzhou. His mother lived in the Fushanli district of Luoyang, and Emperor Taizu often gave her generous gifts and comforted her. After the mother's death, her fate remained unknown.

Tao Sheng, a man from Guozhou, was a young reader and scholar who excelled in poetry. After fifty years, feeling ashamed of his lack of achievement, he sought to join the right-hand position in Guozhou and subsequently became a foot messenger. Guozhou and Shan were part of the state of Kuangguo, and the state's officials appreciated his talents, so they appointed him to Shan. After a long time, at the end of the Jin dynasty, the Khitan invaded Central China. Hou Zhang and Zhao Hui both served as commanders in the state of Kuangguo, and in Shan, Wang Yan served as a leader. A military commander arrived at the inn in Shan, and Hou Zhang and others followed the foreign general to the inn to greet him. Soon, a foreign envoy from the north came and joined them at the inn. The envoy saw Hou Zhang wearing a new brown fur coat and a belt with golden and copper decorations. The envoy repeatedly looked at Hou Zhang and the general, engaging in long conversations. Just before mounting his horse, the foreign general ordered the interpreter to tell Hou Zhang, "The envoy wants the commander's fur coat and belt." The request was made very urgently, and Hou Zhang had no choice but to give them. He borrowed someone else's clothes and belt to return. The three of them walked together, and Hou Zhang went to the south of the ravine and invited Wang Yan and Zhao Hui to drink wine in the camp in the south of the ravine. Afterward, Hou Zhang said, "How can a commander wear such a fur coat?"

The shirt, tied with a copper belt as the main item, could not be taken off the body. Hou Zhang felt angry and frustrated for a long time, and Zhao Hui also became angry, while Wang Yan remained silent. As they were about to disband, Wang Yan said to Hou Zhang and Zhao Hui, "In these troubled times, our clothes and belts are trivial matters. In the future, we don't know where we will die." Hou and Zhao asked, "What should we do?" Wang Yan replied, "To such a situation, what difference does it make? Tonight, leading twenty or thirty people into the inn to cut off the heads of the foreign envoys, and then enter the government office to kill the foreign king's envoys. If we succeed, we will hold on to it; if we fail, we will take our families and loot gold and silk to go to Hedong to seek refuge with Liu the Great King, who is Liu Bang, the founder of the Han dynasty." Hou was initially hesitant and did not respond, but Zhao Hui agreed with him. Wang Yan looked at Hou Zhang for a long time and then left. That night, Wang Yan and Zhao Hui led several dozen brave men into the inn to kill the foreign envoys and take all the valuables back. They then climbed over the wall into the government office, killed the foreign general, and took control of the city. Wang Yan led several hundred armored cavalry to the south of the ravine, intending to kill Hou Zhang. Hou Zhang was terrified and knelt in front of the horse, asking for mercy and instructing him to mount the horse and place Zhao Hui as the leader.

Wang Yan, the Duke of Houzhang, was appointed as a military inspector. He sent Tao Qian and Zhao Yanjin, the son of Zhao Hui, to present a memorial to Han Gaozu, urging him to ascend the throne. Han Gaozu was greatly pleased and rewarded them in sequence. Later, Han Gaozu recognized Wang Yan's contributions, and all three were appointed as high-ranking officials, their deeds recorded in the official histories. Tao Qian then submitted himself to serve Han Gaozu. When the imperial army marched south, he was appointed as an envoy to prepare the ground. After Han Gaozu ascended the throne, Tao Qian was sent to seek Zhao Hui, Wang Yan, and others. The court, having no choice, granted him the position of governor of Guo. However, the officials in power began to watch him closely. He was later demoted to a ceremonial role in the imperial guard and then appointed as a military officer in charge of the auxiliary forces in the northern region. He finally served as the deputy governor of Jingzhou, who also acted as the governor. Tao Qian was skilled in poetry and had a good relationship with Chancellor Pu, often exchanging poems with him. He also served the Hanlin Academy official with the courtesy name "Gu Shu," who was a former military officer in Yan'an and was appointed as a military commander in Huazhou. Tao Qian wrote a farewell letter for his cousin when he was appointed to a military position in Huazhou, stating that the emperor, in the fifth year of his reign, had appointed the former military officer in Yan'an to a military position in Huazhou to ensure peace and order.

The customs of the past have reached the borders of Huai, Ning, and Qin, and the imperial influence has been absent for fifty-nine years. [From the year of Emperor Zhaozong's eastward journey to the present reign of the Holy Court, it has been fifty-nine years. As in the poem by Yu Huan, "Between the borders of Qin and Huai, the imperial influence is far away, and the people of the Southern Xia have long forgotten."] The red horse neighs in the wind, worthy of admiration; the jade toad shines brightly like daylight. Hidden writings of excellence await the discerning reader; climbing a jade tree and laughing at the heavenly breeze. The "Ding" and the "Zhou Song" control the dragon fish and travel through the crimson water. The wide and varied poetry of Han is often not recorded. In his later years, the gentleman recognized the ability of the scholar-official Zhang Yi to write poetry and invited him to his entourage, treating him with great respect. He often said, "In seven-character verse, I am not as good as Zhang Yi; in five-character verse, Zhang Yi is not as good as me." Tao's poem describes a river passing through distant lands and reaching the green mountains of the Han people. Another poem, written during his time in the imperial guard, describes leaving the palace and trying on a simple white robe. There is a collection of Tao's works, with a preface written by the literary master. Zhang Yi often submitted two scrolls of his poetry to the palace teacher Wang Xiang, and Wang Xiang replied with a poem: "The poet from Qinghe, originally a virtuous and talented person, has gifted me sixty new verses. The style and tone are excellent."

宛同罗给事的功夫深厚，如同贾司仓登山才觉天高地广，到海方知浪涛茫茫。好去蟾宫是归路，明年应折桂枝香。陶以副车别驾，权涖藩阃者久之，所至称治，不苛不扰，律身省事而已。在政无赫赫之称，罢任日，民皆攀辕遮留，泣涕塞路，前驱鞭挞之然后进。既遭逢汉祖，始用为开道使，以其读书多学，有木秀之忌，一求荐于三帅，过亦轻矣。授一刺史，二年而罢，竟以散秩坎轲终身，亦命夫。时俗谓之求闗节履捷迳以致身者，得为深诫乎？仲尼曰：“富而可求，虽执鞭之士，吾亦为之。”圣人之旨明，富不可妄求，况贵位乎？子元鼎有文章，擢进士第，乌乎，今不幸而殂矣。

The Ghost of Su Kui's Father in Taihe Su Kui was from Puzhou and had passed the imperial examination for the jinshi degree, ranking second under Emperor Taizong. He served as a high-ranking official in the imperial court and later governed the county of Taihe in the prefecture of Ji. Su Kui's father had passed away several years ago. One day, a military officer from the prefecture of Ji was returning from escorting a convoy to the capital, passing through Huangmei County and staying at an inn. After dusk, an elderly man dressed in black, wearing a short-topped hat, and riding a donkey, accompanied by a servant who was around sixty or seventy years old, arrived at the inn. The elderly man entered the room politely, greeted the military officer, and sat down to talk about visiting relatives and acquaintances in Taihe. The military officer asked, "Who are your relatives and acquaintances in Taihe?" The elderly man replied, "I am from the prefecture of Ji and currently serve in the prefectural office. I am returning from the capital to join my elderly father in the prefecture. We can go together, can we?" He also mentioned that there were no other relatives or acquaintances in Taihe. The military officer said, "The current prefectural magistrate of Taihe is a tongpan (vice-prefect) and is a fellow scholar from the same year as me."

I am happy to serve. Taking the opportunity to send my elderly father to Taihe, I humbly request that you inform the county magistrate of my name. My father has agreed. That evening, the state official ordered wine and we drank together for several cups. The elderly man was very pleased. The next day, we traveled together. Along the way, the state official bought food and we ate together. The elderly man did not hesitate or refuse, and we crossed the river together. We arrived in Jiangzhou, where the elderly man bought wine and invited the state official to drink with him, treating each other with the intimacy of close friends. We reached Hongzhou and stayed together that night. The next day, as we were about to depart, my father said to the state official, "I had intended to accompany you to Taihe, but last night I thought about it and realized that my son, who is already a scholar-official in the capital, will be traveling with his luggage. I have entrusted you to go to Taihe first to inform my son to prepare new clothes and borrow a horse and servant to meet us along the way." The state official of Ji agreed to the request and said, "I will immediately take leave and go home first, not daring to pay a visit to the state official, but first going to Taihe to inform the county magistrate and then presenting a notice to Su Kui. The state official of Ji bowed and rose, quite respectfully, and said that he had come from Huangmei with the respected elders of the outside world."

On the next day after arriving at the county and staying overnight in Hongzhou, the young master's respected father suddenly ordered me to come first to inform the young master to prepare new clothes and borrow a servant and a horse to wait along the way. The young master did not believe it and said, "My father passed away over ten years ago. How could this be?" The governor replied, "We traveled the same road from Huangmei, ate together, and fully explained that the young master went to Taihe especially to visit, which was not false." Su asked about his age, appearance, and body shape, which were identical. He also asked about the wrapping of his clothes, which were also identical. Kui descended from the steps, looked toward his hometown, and cried for a long time. Gradually, he said to the governor, "When my father died, his age, appearance, clothing, and wrapping were no different. After saying this, he cried again." Then he made new clothes, drew a servant and a horse, and burned them. Several years later, Kui also passed away. One might ponder, what kind of creature was the donkey my father rode and the servant he had? They were beings that spoke and ate, but were merely objects possessed by spirits or gods. At the time, I served as the governor of Jizhou, and another official served as the transport commissioner. We had a detailed understanding of the matter and recorded it. Is there any suspicion of talking about strange things?

**The Records of Old News Among the Scholar-Officials of Luoyang, Volume One** **The Complete Library in Four Branches of Literature, Imperially Approved** **The Records of Old News Among the Scholar-Officials of Luoyang, Volume Two** **Compiled by Zhang Qixian of the Song Dynasty** **The Outer Biography of Prince Qi, Zhang Linggong** **Prince Qi, whose name was Quanyi, has a biography in the History of the Five Dynasties. What is recorded here is information outside of that biography, detailing events and observations from the past. Prince Qi was from Puzhou and often served in Huang Chao's army. Knowing that Huang Chao would surely fail, he defected to the state. The Tang dynasty appointed him as the governor of Zhezhou. Upon passing through three cities, he paid homage to the military governor, Zhuge Shuang, who had keen insight into human nature. Seeing Prince Qi's appearance, Zhuge Shuang treated him with great respect and gave him many gifts. Before parting, Zhuge Shuang said to Prince Qi, "One day, your name and position will surpass mine. Strive to achieve this." After Zhuge Shuang's death, Prince Qi gradually gained prominence. He often recalled the kindness shown to him in the past and never forgot to repay it. He had an image of Zhuge Shuang drawn and placed it in his private residence, burning incense before it daily and worshipping it. Every morning, he would light incense in front of the image and only then would he go out to attend to official duties, never showing the slightest slackness throughout his life. His gratitude and loyalty to his roots were evident. He served in Zhezhou for a short time before being transferred to the position of governor of Lüzhou. At that time, Luoyang had been ravaged by war, with counties and towns in ruins, covered in wilderness and thorns, and the fields littered with bones. There were no more inhabitants in the city, which had been completely destroyed by fire. After the chaos caused by Huang Chao and others, three small fortified cities were built to protect the remaining residents and guard against bandits. When Prince Qi first arrived in Luoyang, he took charge and began to restore order.**

Under the command of the general, more than a hundred people, along with only a hundred households in the state, together defended one city in the Central State (Zhongzhou), which is still standing in Luoyang. The term "Central State" was used to refer to the Central Plains region, and "Southern State" was another name for the same area. Prince Qin (Wang) recruited and repaired the city over five or seven years, gradually restoring the magnificent appearance of the capital city, and was in the position of guarding the capital. His biography states that there were fewer than a hundred households in the city of Luoyang, and the biography written by Tang Hong about Prince Qin (Wang) says that within the ruins of broken tiles and shattered earth, it transformed into a capital city. According to the current official history, there are South and North States within the capital city, which were built during the Guangqi period by Zhang Quanyi. During the Tiancheng period of Emperor Mingzong, an imperial decree allowed people to request funds for careful construction, stating that the construction during the Guangqi period was merely a renovation by Prince Qin (Wang), not the initial construction. The city moat still has some remnants. When I was young, I personally heard from the elders that after the Chao-Cai turmoil, there were many cypress trees in the walls of the imperial gardens in Luoyang. When Prince Qin (Wang) repaired the city and the bridges inside and outside, he gathered many cypress trees from nearby imperial gardens and abandoned palaces. In the Holy Court, during the years when there were floods in Luoyang, all the city gates were destroyed. I personally saw piles of timber at the Thick Load and Long Summer gates, most of which were cypress wood, and the temples and bridges in Luoyang were being rebuilt.

The bridge filled with earth is the old bridge on the west canal of Nan Prefecture. The wood obtained was all cypress wood. As the old people said, it can be verified. When Wang first arrived in Luoyang, from among the hundred men under his command, he selected eighteen who could be sent, and appointed them as屯将 (tún jiāng). Each was given a flag and a banner, and ordered them to recruit farmers from the old eighteen counties to cultivate the land themselves. The wandering people gradually returned. Wang then selected another eighteen from among the hundred men to appoint as屯副 (tún fù), and ordered them to pacify the returning people. He punished murderers with death, but others were only flogged. There were no heavy punishments or taxes, and the number of returning people gradually increased. Wang then selected eighteen from among the hundred men to appoint as屯判官 (tún pàn guān), and ordered them to manage the affairs. Within one or two years, each camp increased in households, with the largest having six or seven thousand, the next four thousand, and the smallest three or two thousand. In total, they obtained more than twenty thousand able-bodied men proficient in archery, spear, and sword. If there were bandits, they would immediately capture them. The market and tolls were free of additional taxes and punishments.

The king simplified his affairs, and people from far and near flocked to him like they would to a bustling market. Within five years, the region was hailed as prosperous. He then proposed to each county to appoint a county magistrate and a clerk to oversee local administration. This raises the question: Is it easier to govern after chaos, or did the king find a simple way to govern? With a large population and many households, during the time of teaching and training, Li Han was stationed in Heyang. Li Han was a traitor who frequently attacked the North Mountain's Moyun Fort, earning the nickname "Li Moyun" at the time. He also once established a fort in Luoyang, and to this day, the locals still refer to the area as "Li Moyun Fort." West of the fort is known as "West Fort Market." At that time, Li Han was in control of three cities, and the king focused on teaching the people farming and weaving. He often declared to the public, "What is there to fear from a farmer?" The king disregarded such comments and would send letters to Li Han requesting military provisions and silk cloth. The king said, "Li Taifu's demands must be met," despite his attendants and guests believing it was not advisable to comply. The king replied, "Just give it to him; it seems as though we fear him." His attendants did not understand. Li Han thought the king feared him and was not prepared, so he raised an army to invade Huazhou. The king then secretly summoned his troops, sent them out at night, and by dawn, they had entered the three cities. Li Han, seeing no way to retreat, fled and sought refuge.

In the Hedong region, the court appointed Wang to govern three cities simultaneously and take up the post of governor of the Western Capital. He always appreciated those who worked hard in agriculture and weaving. One year, a family's silkworms and wheat were successful. Within one shè (about 30 kilometers) of the capital, Wang would ride his horse to visit, summon the elderly and close relatives of the family to comfort and reward them, giving them wine, food, and tea. He would give men cloth trousers and women dresses. At the time, the upper class wore blue clothing, and women wore blue silk. He would take new wheat and silk cocoons to see, and his face would show joy when he saw them. People whispered that the king only smiled when he saw good silkworms and wheat, not when he saw beautiful women. His simplicity and honesty were like this. Whenever he saw well-cultivated fields, he would dismount from his horse at the edge of the field and summon the landowner and the people to criticize and punish them if the fields had weeds and the crops were not well-cultivated. If the crops were overgrown and the land was uncultivated, he would question the people, who would say that their oxen were tired or there were not enough people to cultivate the land. He would then dismount from his horse at the edge of the field and summon the neighbors to criticize them, saying, "With so few people and oxen, why didn't you help each other?" The neighbors would admit their fault and he would forgive them. From then on, the people of Luoyang were deeply impressed by his governance.

Regardless of distance, those who have fewer oxen help each other, and those who have fewer people also do so. Farmers and women encourage each other with strength to focus on farming and mulberry cultivation. Therefore, every household has reserves, and even in floods or droughts, there are no starving people. The king was in Luoyang for over forty years and gradually rose to the position of Grand Commandant and Chief of the Secretariat. He was enfeoffed as King of Wei and later moved to be King of Qi. During the reign of Emperor Zhaozong, he performed rituals at the ancestral temple in Luoyang and appointed officials to act as Grand Commandant. At that time, wise officials in the court remarked, "The position of Grand Commandant is a significant office, historically often left vacant, hence the appointment of officials to fill it. Now that King Qi holds the position of Grand Commandant, why is there a need for such an appointment?" The king's sincerity ensured that during floods or droughts, he always took a ritual bath, ate vegetarian food, and slept in a separate room. With great purity and solemnity until reaching the place of worship, dressed in a dignified manner as if facing the highest authority, his demeanor was always respectful. In times of drought, he would pray for rain before it rained, and his attendants would always say, "The king should open the tower." The tower in question is the "Tower of the Fearless Master," located at Guanghua Temple in Longmen. The king would then follow their advice and open the tower. After bowing, he would pray, "Now there is little rain, I fear it will harm the crops. Reverend Master, please show compassion and inform the Buddha to send rain." In this way, it never failed to bring rain. Hence, there was a folk saying at the time: "When King Qi prays for rain, it rains." Is it the pure and sincere heart of King Qi that has moved the fearless deity? King Qi returned to the army at Chao first.

Li Keyong was appointed as the governor of Zezhou by the Tang dynasty and later returned to the Tang dynasty to be appointed as the governor of Tongzhou. Afterward, Li Cunxiao served as Chancellor and Grand Secretary alongside Li Keyong, and Li Keyong governed four regions. Li Cunxiao repeatedly submitted memorials to decline the additional post, as he secretly recognized Li Keyong as a cunning and powerful figure and wished to avoid his power and position in order to plan for self-preservation. Li Keyong built up a dominant empire, with frequent wars externally and an often-empty state treasury internally. Li Cunxiao dedicated all his efforts and exhausted all resources to assist him. When Li Keyong lost a northern campaign, he became suspicious and jealous of Li Cunxiao, fearing future trouble. He repeatedly tried to kill him several times, but thanks to Lady Cui, who confronted Li Keyong publicly, Li Cunxiao was spared. It was also because Li Cunxiao was loyal and straightforward, without any duplicity, and had many meritorious deeds throughout the world, so he could not be swayed by them. Li Keyong then arranged for his son, the Prince of Fu, to marry Li Cunxiao's daughter. For this reason, although Li Cunxiao had dedicated himself to Li Keyong, Emperor Zhuangzong (Li Cunxiao) always harbored resentment toward Li Cunxiao. When Emperor Zhuangzong conquered the Later Liang dynasty, Li Cunxiao submitted a petition to await punishment, and Emperor Zhuangzong issued an edict to pardon him. Later, he called for a meeting and was very pleased, opening his heart and comforting him as if seeing a lifelong friend, expressing all the feelings of a fish and water. They discussed contemporary affairs, and everything Li Cunxiao said exceeded the expectations of Emperor Zhuangzong's meritorious ministers, whom he regretted getting.

In his later years, his intelligence, modesty, virtue, and reputation were so impressive that they moved the emperor. He then submitted another memorial, recounting how he had repeatedly been targeted by Zhu Wen of Later Liang, narrowly escaping danger, and being forced into a marriage alliance, which was not part of his original aspirations. He requested that the emperor clear his name, and in his memorial, he wrote: "I humbly reflect that I once dwelt in a foul tree and drank from a stolen spring, truly bearing blemishes that have not yet been cleared. Your Majesty's grand decree has again issued an edict to clear my name." The Empress Liu was then ordered to visit the Crown Prince's residence to console him. The Empress insisted on paying homage to the Crown Prince and his consort, Crown Prince Consort, but the Crown Prince avoided meeting her, fearing to do so. Upon returning to the palace, the Empress reported this to the emperor and said, "In my youth, I lost my parents and wish to take the Crown Prince and Crown Prince Consort as adoptive parents." Emperor Zhuangzong agreed to this request. The Crown Prince repeatedly submitted memorials to decline the honor but eventually accepted it. Emperor Zhuangzong then ordered the Hanlin Academy and the Ministry of Rites to draft the protocols for the Empress and the Crown Prince Consort to meet as adoptive parents and for their subsequent correspondence. This was an unprecedented event in history. Wang Yanhan, the Middle Commandant of the Central Commandery, whose father, Wang Yanhan, was a military officer in Henan, was preparing to take the imperial examinations. His father took the opportunity to inform the Crown Prince, saying, "My son has a rough aptitude for literature; he is now being urged by his peers to take the exams. I await your instructions." The Crown Prince replied, "If he wishes to take the exams, bring the scrolls to me, and I will consider it."

A Scholar's Father Greets with Respect A scholar, upon arriving, greeted the father of another scholar by bowing twice. After returning home, his son presented several scrolls of written material early in the morning. Wang, a high-ranking official, ordered the father of the scholar to instruct someone to hurry up the steps. Wang said, "It is not advisable to hurry up the steps; once a person becomes a candidate for the imperial examinations, they should return to their official duties." Wang then advised Wei Gong's father, "Different paths should not be interfered with; ultimately, they should be treated with guest-like courtesy." Upon meeting, Wang was very surprised and treated the person with great respect. In that year, Wang strongly advocated to contemporary Confucian scholars and recommended someone, leading to their promotion to the highest rank. When the emperor of the Jin dynasty was in power, Wei Gong, a noble in office, submitted a petition that the shrine dedicated to a prince in Luoyang lacked a name and requested the title "Zhongsu Gong." The emperor agreed, but the decree was not implemented due to court affairs. The emperor knew that the prince had great achievements at the end of the Tang dynasty, and the people of Luoyang had been benefited for forty years. The emperor believed that it was appropriate to establish a shrine and ordered the officials to use the title "Zhongsu Gong" for the shrine. The stone tablets and pavilions commemorating the noble's virtues were rebuilt. It is known that great achievements will surely be recognized. The noble was deceased during the Tang dynasty but became prominent during the Song dynasty. If the current emperor restores the shrine and temple, then the noble's achievements will remain immortal even after a thousand years.

Mr. Li, the Junior Tutor to the Crown Prince, and His Virtuous Wife During the late Tang Dynasty, Mr. Li, whose posthumous name was Su, served as a high-ranking official and was known for his talents and integrity. He was from the Western Capital (Chang'an) and was appointed by Prince Qi, who was the guardian of the Western Capital and also controlled Henan (河阳). Prince Qi, recognizing Mr. Li's exceptional qualities, gave his daughter, the legitimate wife of Prince Qi, to him in marriage. This daughter was the offspring of the noblewoman known as Lady Xianyi. After several years, she passed away. Prince Qi then gave another daughter, born to one of his concubines, to Mr. Li in marriage. Although this second daughter was not the offspring of Lady Xianyi, she was intelligent and skilled in many arts. Prince Qi and Lady Xianyi cherished her more than their first daughter. She excelled in music and needlework, and her knowledge and beauty were almost that of a celestial being. She was virtuous, wise, and observant of propriety, and maintained a diligent demeanor throughout her life. When her husband became noble and was enfeoffed as Lady of Qinghe County, she managed the household very strictly. The household had numerous concubines and servants, and the couple had separate quarters. Mr. Li's quarters had dozens of concubines, and the lady also had dozens of concubines. She secretly ordered spies to monitor the husband's quarters to ensure that the concubines did not slightly disobey his commands. If they did, she would call them and beat them, then choose beautiful and young ones to replace them. Every year on the husband's birthday, she would first keep young girls who understood music, adorn them with jade, pearls, and embroidered fabrics, and then hold the cup, wish him long life, and present ornaments and playthings together. Sometimes, she would refuse because there were many concubines and servants, and then again choose the ordinary ones to return to her own quarters. She would serve them for a long time and marry them off when the time was right. When the husband entered the court and was about to return, she would prepare a skirt and veil to await him.

Beside the central hall, the official ordered a young servant to investigate. Upon meeting, he was treated with the full ceremonial respect due to a guest. If he was tired, he would leave immediately after a brief meeting. If the meeting was more leisurely, music was played to welcome and escort him back. When he entered the courtyard, fresh fruits and wine were prepared, and they talked about current and past events. He loved and feared him, never daring to show any displeasure in front of him. Li Gong once was sent to manage the salt interests in An邑 and Jie县. Upon arriving, he encountered a mutiny by the garrison soldiers. Taking advantage of the situation, he promised a large sum of money from the treasury and only punished the main instigators. The rebels were thus dispersed, and dozens of those involved were executed. The people's hearts were immediately calmed. At that time, a powerful person who had a close relationship with the official asked for a cherry orchard in Luoyang but was refused, creating a rift. He often sought to harm Li Gong. Therefore, he secretly reported, saying, "Li Gong illegally used public funds to gain fame and seek extraordinary rewards." Thus, the censor was ordered to investigate, and the case was very urgent. It was like bait in a tiger's mouth. The official's wife heard about it and rode in a sedan chair directly to the palace gate, waiting for the powerful person to come out and bow on the side of the road. Soon, she knocked on the horse's reins with a loud voice, weeping and pleading, citing several examples from ancient and modern times to prove the consequences of honor, disgrace, fortune, misfortune, success, and failure. Her sadness and grief moved everyone around, and the powerful person felt ashamed.

Deeply regretting, he turned back and returned to court, requesting an audience out of turn, arguing in a roundabout way to clear his name. He also mentioned that he had a wife named Zhang, who was the daughter of the Prince of Qi. She approached his horse, weeping and pleading. The emperor heard about this and was shocked, asking, "Is this a virtuous woman?" He immediately ordered her to be sent out by fast relay, and Li Gong thus avoided disaster. Until the Jin Dynasty, the Khitan prince Dongdan died by misfortune, and the Khitans had already known about it. Li Gong was ordered to escort Dongdan's coffin back to the northern court. After returning home, he was filled with worry and despair, not knowing what to do, and only stayed outside the city, sitting alone for a long time. His wife was surprised by his behavior and ordered the servants to invite him. Once inside, she asked Li Gong, "Is there something about your mission that is unsatisfactory?" He silently wept and said, "I am old, and my children are young, and I am weeping and choking, unable to speak further." She replied, "Is it not that you are being sent to a distant land? If that is the case, you should not cry like children." Li Gong wept bitterly and said, "Now I am ordered to send Dongdan to the north, and the court has secretly planned to harm him, which the Khitans have already known. I am not afraid of the long journey, but I will surely not return." She said, "But if you think about it, the northern people are greedy for profit. The jewels and gold in my house can amount to tens of thousands, which I can give to the king's attendants, and the rest can be offered as tribute, ensuring your safety."

Deeply regretting, he turned back and returned to court, requesting an audience out of turn, arguing in a roundabout way to clear his name. He also mentioned that he had a wife named Zhang, who was the daughter of the Prince of Qi. She approached his horse, weeping and pleading. The emperor heard about this and was shocked, asking, "Is this a virtuous woman?" He immediately ordered her to be sent out by fast relay, and Li Gong thus avoided disaster. Until the Jin Dynasty, the Khitan prince Dongdan died by misfortune, and the Khitans had already known about it. Li Gong was ordered to escort Dongdan's coffin back to the northern court. After returning home, he was filled with worry and despair, not knowing what to do, and only stayed outside the city, sitting alone for a long time. His wife was surprised by his behavior and ordered the servants to invite him. Once inside, she asked Li Gong, "Is there something about your mission that is unsatisfactory?" He silently wept and said, "I am old, and my children are young, and I am weeping and choking, unable to speak further." She replied, "Is it not that you are being sent to a distant land? If that is the case, you should not cry like children." Li Gong wept bitterly and said, "Now I am ordered to send Dongdan to the north, and the court has secretly planned to harm him, which the Khitans have already known. I am not afraid of the long journey, but I will surely not return." She said, "But if you think about it, the northern people are greedy for profit. The jewels and gold in my house can amount to tens of thousands, which I can give to the king's attendants, and the rest can be offered as tribute, ensuring your safety."

He also mentioned that he had a wife named Zhang, who was the daughter of the Prince of Qi. She approached his horse, weeping and pleading.
