The jewels and gold in my house can amount to tens of thousands, which I can give to the king's attendants, and the rest can be offered as tribute, ensuring your safety."

Li Gong returned quickly out of fear of receiving a generous return gift, following his advice, went to the Khitan state, bribed the attendants, used all his resources, and gave them as personal gifts. The Khitan ruler and ministers were indeed greatly pleased, ordered to send him back quickly, bestowed hundreds of named horses, also bestowed hundreds of camels, and clothing and utensils were commensurate with the horses and camels. Upon returning, he dared not keep them, presented them all, as a result, he received a generous official reward, it was the wife's effort. Before this, Zhao Siwan was in Yongxing, sent the head to court, Zhao Siwan was the deputy governor of Lantian, had already been found guilty. Li Gong was at the time a military commander and the supervisor of the three white channels, also the deputy governor of the military affairs, protected himself and got him out. Came to thank Li Gong, upon returning home, the wife questioned him, "Zhao Siwan is an ordinary and lowly person, why did you spare him from his fault? He has already come to thank you, so why did you see him again?" Li Gong replied, "I did not say anything before, but now that you ask, I must speak. This Zhao Siwan, though lowly, upon careful observation, truly appears like a traitor and a villain. I regret that there is no evidence to remove him." The wife said, "Since we cannot remove him, why not bribe him with small favors? So that he does not harbor resentment, the wife later secretly sent someone to invite Zhao Siwan's wife, gave her generous gifts of clothing, and gave him a lot of money and goods."

During the Han Dynasty, when high-ranking generals retired and returned to Yong, not long after, Si Wan closed the city gates and rebelled. Many of the gentry class suffered greatly, but the entire family of the general escaped disaster. Eventually, through strategy, the general persuaded Si Wan to surrender, and the city was taken by Zhou Zu, who had known the general's name all along and brought him back to the imperial court. The general changed his official position and retired to Luoyang, also due to the efforts of his wife. Moreover, it is normal for women to be stingy with wealth and jealous, but those who are not jealous and not stingy with wealth are rare. In a well-governed era, it is difficult to confront powerful officials face-to-face and advocate for ancient virtues, protecting the virtuous, as disaster comes quickly. The proof of retribution is evident, even for brave men who are willing to risk their lives, are hesitant to do so, let alone women. Not only did she clear her husband's guilt and save the entire family from disaster, but she was also a woman who treated her husband with great respect, as in the famous anecdote of "holding a tray like a guest." Was she not virtuous? Was she not virtuous? How can she be compared to women who adorn themselves with makeup and play with their eyebrows, seduce their husbands, and secretly gain high status? The deeds of virtuous women who can serve as models of motherhood are numerous, but my eyesight has dimmed, my steps are heavy, and my strength has diminished. I will briefly mention some outstanding and unusual cases in a chapter, ensuring that her good name remains unblemished for a thousand years. I was a guest at the door of Li Gong for a long time, so I heard the story in great detail.

**Translation:** A Record of Strange Events in Fangzhou In the following year after I was in charge of transportation in Jiangnan, there were bandits named Liu Fading and his relatives, brothers of eight, all skilled in martial arts and archery. Liu Fading was the leader of the bandits, and his followers numbered almost a hundred. The state and prefecture were troubled by them and reported it to Emperor Taizong. The emperor ordered the two road inspectors to eliminate them together. Their followers then dispersed. At that time, Shi Yi was the western road inspector, and the government troops also suffered many casualties due to Liu Fading's bandits. I found Liu Fading's fellow villager Xu Man, who was young and close to him. Xu Man was strong and energetic, able to travel hundreds of miles a day. He often served as a retainer and returned home on leave. I sent Xu Man to persuade them, promising amnesty and rewards in exchange for their submission. I set a deadline of ten days and first ordered them to come to Fangzhou as agreed. The transport official used a single sheet to invite them, and they all came to confess their crimes. They conspired with the governor Yin Qi and the deputy governor Li Su to kill them all.

"之" protected "戎韓宗祐" and did not allow "許懷琪" to proceed, while "尹玘" secretly submitted a memorial to report the matter. He also stated that this bandit's brothers were exceptionally brave and daring. The old gang had scattered and hidden in the mountains; if there was a sudden flood or drought, they would gather and become a threat to the state. He requested severe punishment to kill them, and the court approved the request. "法定" and his seven brothers were nailed alive in the market for several days. When "許懷琪" passed by, "法定" and his brothers all shouted and cursed, saying that the officials had betrayed them. The transport commissioner had promised them life, but "康怀琪" and the governor had conspired against them, causing their brothers to suffer unjust deaths. In the afterlife, they would surely accuse "許怀琪" and never let his sins go unpunished. Furious, "許怀琪" ordered his attendants to crush their hands and feet with iron hammers, and thus the eight men died instantly, their bodies being thrown outside the city. Less than half a year later, when "許怀琪" returned from reporting official business in the capital, he sailed upstream to Fuzhou. As he and "許怀琪" bowed and greeted each other, he repeatedly bowed and said, "I am guilty." He explained that he had temporarily left Hongzhou to go to the capital and then returned to Jiangnan, and he had not known about "法定's" death. He was surprised by "許怀琪's" sincere expression of guilt and said, "Let us sit down; I have heard this before."

称罪过者何怀琪又起面若死灰且战且惧惟言某罪过覩之愈惊疑未测何故也遂答以他事无何郡长与州从事皆至促船夫疾牵至州部到驿诸官悉散去余未及解带怀琪独候谒未及与接谈又再三言某罪过似有所依慿及去召驿吏及州之走使辈诘之皆曰巡检寻常不如此得非为刘法定兄弟寃魂所使尔不然何恐惧称罪之若是因问法定等今何在遂以怀琪所谋事对余亦惘然嗟叹者久之余在防州数日欲往大庾县数处勾当当申酉间郡长与康俱在坐余告以起发之由且请诸公不得出门俟昏晩上马尹公等送至城门独怀琪先辞而退门外余俟开鎻讫上马南去行三十许里闻 Translation: When someone accused of a crime, Huaiqi, appeared pale and fearful, constantly repeating the accusation, I was increasingly surprised and uncertain about the reason. He responded with other matters, and soon the county magistrate and the state official arrived, urging the boatman to hurry and take us to the state office. Upon arrival at the posthouse, all the officials had already dispersed. I hadn't even had time to undo my belt when Huaiqi waited alone to greet me, but I didn't get a chance to talk with him. He repeatedly mentioned the crime, as if he were relying on something. After he left, I summoned the posthouse officials and the state runners to question them, and they all said that the inspector usually didn't act this way. Could it be that the spirits of Liu Ding's brothers were causing this? Otherwise, why would he be so fearful and keep accusing himself? I then asked where Liu Ding and his brothers were, and they replied with what Huaiqi had plotted. I was also bewildered and sighed for a long time. I stayed in Fangzhou for several days, planning to go to Dayu County to handle some matters. During the late afternoon to evening, the county magistrate and Kang were both present. I informed them of my departure plans and asked them not to leave the city until late at night. The officials, including Yin Gong, saw me off to the city gate, while Huaiqi left early. Outside the gate, I waited until it was unlocked, then mounted my horse and headed south. After traveling about thirty miles, I heard...

Those who ride horses press against each other, and I command the attendants to investigate. They say that Kang, the patrol inspector, arrives hesitantly with Hua Qi. I question him, and he explains that just now he had already declined to stay but is now coming to accompany me to Dayu County. We set off together, and the next day we arrive at the Dayu County inn. The inn has two rooms, one to the east and one to the west; I stay in the left room, and Kang stays in the right room. As evening approaches, I order us to eat together. After walking several hundred steps, his demeanor is as usual, and he says nothing else. As dusk approaches, he bows and withdraws. I am also tired and so take off my clothes and lie down to sleep. I am in a daze, as if in a dream, and I see old acquaintances who have been dead for over ten years. He repeatedly bids farewell with tears of affection, and suddenly, I no longer see him. I wake up and find it strange. Suddenly, I hear someone calling my attendants, and the voice is quite urgent. I am startled awake and ask who it is. It turns out to be Hua Qi's deputy, who says that Kang, the patrol inspector, has suddenly fallen ill and wishes to leave. I hurry to Kang's place, where he immediately holds his knees and groans, saying his leg pain is unbearable. He has already ordered a small boat to be prepared to return downstream to Fangzhou to seek medical help. Several people support him to the boat, and I walk with a cane following them. Kang turns his head and leaves with a sad cry, similar to the one who bid farewell to me in my dream. After several more days, I continue my journey.

Traveling by boat from Dayu to Fangzhou, I quickly asked the patrol officer about his safety, and he replied that he had died two nights ago. Not long after, Han the Imperial Attendant Jingyou arrived and fully described how Huaiqi, when he was still alive, had hair styled like a strong man, with the hairdo bent backward, and the chin pointing upward, with the hairdo on the back of the neck. His breathing was very coarse, and it required the effort of three or four people pushing from behind to lift his head. He only said, "guilt, guilt," and when soup was brought to his mouth, it was as if someone was swatting it away, and it all fell to the ground. Though very hungry and thirsty, he only emptied the vessel and withdrew, except for saying "guilt," he had no other words until his death. Within the year, Yin Qi also died. The Deputy Prefect Li Su originally did not agree with their plan but signed along. Later, he also suffered from a heart disease and could not personally manage the market and customs duties outside, barely being a complete person. Different from Feideng and others, who originally committed murder and robbery, killing many people, the crime was already deep. One who was the ringleader and was punished, then suffered an unjust death, still has a spirit like this, how much more so for those who kill the innocent? Record it as a warning for future generations. Han Jingyou, who knows literature and has insight, has repeatedly been entrusted by the state to record this matter and show it to others.

The Magistrate's Wife of Hengyang: A Tale of Retribution The magistrate of Hengyang, whose name is lost to history, once visited a man from Shu (Sichuan). A few years after losing his first wife, he remarried, and his new wife was also from Shu. This new wife bore three daughters, all of whom grew up and came to live with the magistrate. He treated them as if they were his own daughters. However, his new wife was cruel and jealous. He had several old female servants, among whom two were pregnant. Every day, his new wife would beat them for other reasons. The two servants, while pregnant, often received beatings on their backs, buttocks, or legs. The magistrate's wife tried various ways to hit their bellies to make sure they would not have full-term pregnancies. Eventually, the two servants miscarried and died from the beatings. At that time, I was serving as a deputy governor in Jizhou and often heard of the injustice. After I resigned from my position, I returned home and learned that the magistrate's family had not been heard from for a long time. An old friend who knew the magistrate mentioned the story and said: "After the magistrate's wife killed the two servants, the three daughters married and temporarily stayed in Hengyang. Not more than four or five years later, all three daughters died during childbirth. Every time one of the daughters died, his wife would first feed monks and repent. The two servants who died from miscarriages would read sutras and perform self-punishment like Emperor Wu of Liang, and the two daughters who died during childbirth would also perform self-punishment. Before they died, they would cry and suffer for days before finally passing away. The magistrate's wife cried and read sutras, confessing her sins to the Buddha, burning incense, and becoming distressed, which led to her illness. A year after the daughters died, she also passed away." Alas! The saying in the *Book of Han* states, "The heavenly net is vast but leaves no gaps." The Buddhist teachings of retribution are so clear and evident. Writing this down serves to warn jealous and cruel women to increase their fear and to serve as a form of admonition that benefits the world.

The Record of Old News of the Scholars of Luoyang, Volume Two <Subclass: Miscellaneous, Subcategory: Anecdotes, Title: The Record of Old News of the Scholars of Luoyang> The Complete Library in Four Treasuries The Record of Old News of the Scholars of Luoyang, Volume Three By Zhang Qixian of the Song Dynasty Duke Xiang's Relocation to Righteousness Duke Xiang, whose given name was Gong, has a biography in the official history. This account supplements what is missing from the official records. Duke Xiang was a bold, versatile, and cunning man, brave, decisive, and firm, truly a hero. When he was young, he was skilled in archery, hitting the target eight or nine times out of ten. He was born in Fenyang and moved with his father to Lu. At around twenty years old, he had uncommon courage and spirit. He valued his word highly, was generous with wealth, and admired righteousness. He liked to be a patron of the wayward and associated with outlaws, doing nothing he did not do. He often had an affair with a married woman from Lu. After half a year, Duke Xiang told the woman he was having an affair with, "I haven't seen your husband for many days. What happened?" The woman laughed and said, "Because of our affair, he often grinds a dagger to kill me, afraid you won't get the chance. Fearing you won't catch up to my house soon, I conspired with a neighbor's son to offer tens of thousands of coins to hire someone to kill him." The neighbor's son said, "If I kill him, will you marry me?" Thinking that her husband often wanted to kill her and she had no way to escape, she agreed. When her husband was drunk and sleeping outside the city, the neighbor's son secretly killed him and buried him, afraid that people would find out and he would have to flee. Duke Xiang asked, "Where is the neighbor's son now?" The woman said, "He is at a certain place." Duke Xiang secretly found him and killed him. He then returned and blamed the woman he was having an affair with, saying, "You are having an affair with someone and harming your husband, which is unjust. Your husband's death is due to..."

I cannot kill the woman immediately; I cannot throw her head into the marketplace and declare, "I killed this woman," then calmly wave my arm and walk away. The patrol officer admired me and feared my courage and strength, so he dared not pursue me. Therefore, I fled to avoid punishment until amnesty was granted and I returned. My father's worry was visible on his face. He was a virtuous and upright man, and he was friendly with the former chief justice, Lord Teng. At the time, Lord Teng was still a commoner. I then sought advice from Lord Teng, saying, "What method can I use to save my son from destroying our family?" Lord Teng replied, "Daring to provoke a tiger by pulling its whiskers to hasten disaster." The father said to him, "My son, although like this, his nature is also easy to understand." Last year, there was a scholar over fifty years old, tall and strong but destitute, who sat with his legs spread out like a箕 (a winnowing basket) beside my gate. My son returned from outside, looked at the scholar intently, but the scholar did not look at him. My son then went out again, scolded the scholar, but the scholar just sat there and did not move. He slowly said, "What kind of person are you to dare to insult me? If it were so, then I am fortunate to wear the scholar's robe and follow the scholar's conduct. If it were twenty years ago when I did not know how to read, you would have been crushed like mustard powder." Then he stood up, grabbed his clothes, and showed my son his hands, saying, "See this? See how his muscles are strong and fierce like a wolf or tiger." He said, "Let you go, let you go." My son heard this and happily said, "Truly a great man."

At first, someone called the common scholar and sat with legs spread wide, looking down arrogantly, and was so disrespectful that he easily insulted and wanted to punch and beat him. Upon hearing the words of the scholar and seeing his aspirations, he then apologized, saying, "It was my fault, it was my fault." Then he invited him in and ordered wine and food. The scholar gradually saw the son's demeanor, bearing, and speech, and realized that he was truly a bold young man of that time. The scholar especially treated the son with courtesy, and the son also lowered himself and bowed before leaving. From this perspective, it is easy to understand. The father returned and told the county magistrate, "Mr. Teng is truly a distinguished scholar; the whole county respects him. I saw that you were acting in a way that could lead to entanglement in a net, why didn't you go to see him?" The county magistrate went at night under the moon and knocked on Mr. Teng's door, inviting him in and saying, "Yesterday, my father told me to go and pay respects to the scholar." Mr. Teng responded to all my questions with reason, and was a truly remarkable scholar. Mr. Teng thought highly of him, secretly wondering why he was tainted by violence and brutality. Then he said to the county magistrate, "I do not know your son; he is treated as a bandit or a thug in the region of Lu. Now that I see your talent and appearance, you are truly a noble person. Please take care of yourself and do not associate with the wrong kind of people. Recall the ancient cases where people were wrongfully treated as bandits. If they changed their ways, they gained eternal fame like Zhou Chu." The county magistrate, upon hearing this, could not help but lean forward, and the conversation ended. The county magistrate then said, "From now on, I wish to serve you as a younger brother."

The scholar had completely changed what he used to do. Upon returning home, he bowed to his father and said, "Although my parents gave me life, it was Master Teng who taught me, and it was Master Teng who saved my life." He told his father everything about how he treated Master Teng as a younger brother. His father, upon hearing this, was very pleased. From then on, he politely declined his old friends who were once bold and heroic, and frequently visited Master Teng. Within a few years, people in Lu who knew him all said, "This is the thousand-mile horse of the Xiang family." He dressed like a scholar, his demeanor and bearing were calm and elegant, and he did not associate with people of low character. Whenever he heard of people with virtue, knowledge, and skills, he often visited them. Soon after, his father passed away, and he bid farewell to his relatives in Lu, with the ambition to travel and explore. He visited many officials and nobles, but they all considered him an ordinary person, so he left. He then served Lord Yi for less than half a year and departed again. He heard that Emperor Gaozu had established a powerful base and wanted to join him. Coincidentally, there was a famine, and the roads were filled with bandits and robbers. He planned to enter the Hedong region through Shifang Pass. At that time, Chang Shizhong, a high-ranking official named Guo Xun, was the governor of Shifang Pass and responsible for customs and markets. Guo Xun was knowledgeable and had good judgment. The scholar visited him, and Guo Xun kept him for more than a month. Guo Xun said, "Now bandits and robbers are rampant on the roads. You, with a young servant and two donkeys, wearing fine clothes and looking well-groomed, do not seem like someone in poverty. The dangerous journey is not a good path to take. And I have recently heard..."

A bandit lived a few miles away from the town, frequently practicing with bows and crossbows, leaving no travelers unharmed. After waiting for a few days, he had companions numbering in the dozens. I also gathered the town's able-bodied men to escort you out of the pass, saying that the road ahead was not a place to linger and advised you to take good care of yourself. After departing, we did not travel more than thirty miles when we encountered a group of dozens of bandits on the roadside. They shot arrows at us. I went directly to the bandits, greeted them all, and then introduced myself by name, stating that I was a military officer on duty, without a master to rely on, now going to Hedong to seek employment, with one servant and two donkeys in tow, clothing and a few coins for expenses, and no other valuables. Recently, I learned that those who have gone ahead were all robbed and stripped. I thanked you for your kindness and asked you to trouble three or five of you to escort me further. Among them, one with a long beard, large face, strong and agile appearance, and a commanding presence smiled and looked at his companions, saying, "Look at this person daring to ask us to escort him, what's the matter?" One of them said, "He has a bow and arrow, let's ask him to shoot an arrow, what do you think?" The bearded man said to the central commander, "Brothers, just watch me shoot with the bow and arrow." I humbly declined for a long time, but the bandits insisted. If I had to, I took the bow and arrow, and all the arrows were missing their fletching, about half of them.

A group of bandits, about an inch in height, were amazed and stayed to talk, saying, "Will you be staying here for three or four days?" They said, "Allow us brothers to take charge of the matter. We will return to the pass." Guo Xun was surprised and said they were afraid to proceed. They told him the reason. The next day, at dusk, the bandit leader indeed sent someone to invite the speaker. The speaker followed them and left the town, about six or seven li into a dense forest, where they set up a banquet. The utensils were all made of white gold. They lit the fire and roasted the meat. They sat down and offered wine to the speaker. The speaker said, "I never drink alcohol." The bandit leader also did not press him. After they finished eating, they ordered someone to take the road to send them off. Soon, a bandit came with a silver bar and led a horse. The saddle and bridle were fresh and neat. The bandit leader said, "You have no horse; let me provide you with a substitute. The silver will be used to pay for tea and soup in Hedong." The speaker accepted everything and formed an alliance. The bandit leader then sent ten thieves to escort the speaker to his destination. He said to the speaker, "These are all drivers. If you are not satisfied, they will be severely beaten." The speaker left the next day and eventually reached Jin Yang, where Han Zu had risen to prominence. At that time, Zhou Zu was still serving as a subordinate officer. Han Zu had placed his trust in him, and his influence had grown significantly. At that time, Zhou Zu returned home late and hurriedly left the road.

**Translation:** The person held a petition, stated their name, and prostrated themselves before Zhou Zu. Zhou Zu looked at them for a long time. When he returned home, he called them in and asked about the purpose of their visit. They fully explained their intentions and said, "I have no followers in the world and wish to serve you closely." Zhou Zu replied, "I am already in the service of the Great King; how dare I retain you?" The person had previously spoken boldly and sincerely, and Zhou Zu felt compassion for them. Every day, they waited for Zhou Zu to return and prostrated themselves as before. One day, Zhou Zu invited them into his residence and said, "I am concerned that daily prostrations might be seen as excessive and could cause trouble if discovered by the king. Since the secretary is a guest, it would be better for you to stay here with me, and I will accompany you; you repeatedly expressed gratitude." From then on, they moved into Zhou Zu's residence. When Zhou Zu returned, they ate together, and the respectful treatment was very courteous. Zhou Zu highly valued them. Liu Gaozu raised troops to the south and appointed Zhou Zu as the Deputy Chief Military Councillor by imperial decree. Zhou Zu and the person began to fully observe the rituals of service. When Han Zu became emperor, Zhou Zu was appointed as the Chief Military Councillor. Zhou Zu was appointed as the Chief of the Imperial Household and the Master of Ceremonies. When Zhou Zu entered the pass and pacified three rebellions in Qishan, Bao, and Yong, the Chief of the Imperial Household accompanied him and contributed significantly with secret plans and strategies. When the army returned, Zhou Zu remained in charge of the capital city of Ye, holding the rank of Chief Military Councillor, with a large force of infantry and cavalry.
