During the time of the Tang dynasty, there was an official named Zheng Ma who managed affairs at an extraordinary level, often involving himself in decisions alongside the central command. Emperor Gaozu of Han entrusted him with all matters, regardless of their size. When the young emperor of Han secretly plotted against Zhou Zu, Zhou Zu became aware of the plot and was supported by the three armies, who faced the imperial palace with their forces. Up to the point of abdication, the central command played a significant role. During the reign of Emperor Shizong, Zheng Ma led battles in Qin and Feng, defeated the cavalry and infantry commanders He Jin and Fan Ainen, and secured the surrender of hundreds more, all of which were due to the efforts of the central command. As a result, Emperor Shizong's military prestige was greatly enhanced, and the territories in the north and south were expanded, with no obstacle in front. All of the regions from Huainan to the south and Bashang became part of the empire. Zheng Ma served as the South Institute Envoy, held the symbols of authority in the region, and later became the governor of the western capital, reaching the rank of Chancellor. After his death, his reputation remained intact throughout his life, and his achievements were prominent, making him the only one among the nobility of recent times. In Luoyang, he delegated political affairs to his attendants, planted bamboo and trees, allowed himself to indulge in music and entertainment, and did not accumulate wealth. After his death, his descendants were in poverty within a decade. He valued talented people, treated others openly without suspicion, did not unjustly punish or disturb the people, and had great achievements in the world. He never boasted about his achievements, and these were things that ancient princes and kings found difficult to do. He was a Counselor of State named Teng Gong.

讳中正，上党人，博通经史，谨厚寡言。五常百行，无所亏缺，长于时务，清俭率下，风规肃然，有古人风，不茍进取，守命俟时而已。会向中令遇周祖，既而思曩昔之事，曰：“若非滕公，吾为伏莽辈所污死矣。今日立身荣贵，忝滕公之力也。”使人延请奏于周祖。由是向中令屡立方面大勲，滕公为賔佐，悉従行。向中令以功名终始者，滕公之助也。向居守洛阳，为府判官。向中令镇安陆，滕公授仓部员外郎。后大谏议，执宪纲久之，寿终于洛阳私第。余在洛阳布衣时，滕公时为府判官，受滕公知，为江南转运使，蒙滕公举，目覩中令之美，耳闻滕公之説甚详。故书俟他日取中令传校之，传之详者去之，传之略者存之，冀有补于太史氏而已。

The Lady of Zhang Xiang: From Misfortune to Prosperity The Lady of Zhang Xiang, whose name is not recorded, was from Hedong. She was beautiful, intelligent, and had many skills. At the age of fourteen or fifteen, she lost her chastity to a military officer and became his concubine. When the military officer was replaced and returned to Luoyang, she came with him. They traveled to Shanxi, where she fell ill. She was carried there, and upon reaching Beixiaojì (a place name), her illness was severe, and she could not even drink soup. From morning to evening, she had diarrhea more than a hundred times. Her body was emaciated, and the stench was unbearable. The military officer was disgusted with her and abandoned her on the road to Zhou. She did not eat for several days, and passersby were moved to pity. By the roadside, there was a cave that could hold several people, a place where woodcutters and shepherds took shelter from wind and rain. Passersby felt sorry for her and carried her to the cave. After several more days, her diarrhea gradually improved, but her clothes were all taken by robbers. She only covered herself with a broken mat and some grass. Gradually, she walked to a shop and begged for food every day, and at night, she stayed at an inn.

Under the eaves, one day an old woman said to him, "Seeing that you are not a beggar, my residence is not far, about three hundred steps away, and I will take you there." The old woman bathed and clothed him with her own clothes, feeding him only porridge, soup, and vegetable rice. After not many months, he recovered completely, and his appearance became beautiful and radiant, almost like a fairy. Local men with sons came to propose marriage, and they all wanted to marry her, but Zhang rejected all of them. Suddenly, a scholar passed by and heard about it, and he insisted on meeting her. After meeting, he told the old woman that he could marry her and would give her fifty bolts of silk. The old woman agreed, and he exchanged her for new clothes and jewelry, which he loaded onto a cart and left. The scholar then took her to Xiangyang and rented a house to live in. When the governor of Xiangyang, Prince An, rebelled, his subordinates, seeing the scholar's wealth, killed him and took his wife. After Prince An was defeated, he was captured by rebel soldiers, and someone who recognized her beauty sent her to the camp of Zhang, the Grand Councillor. Zhang, who was also known as From En, gathered a total of many women.

十余 years ago, Zhang Xiang favored and treated the wife of a scholar deeply. When Zhang's legitimate wife passed away, he took the scholar's wife as his second wife. She was later enfeoffed as a County Lady and, when she became the mistress of the household, she was adept at managing the home, especially strict and orderly, always adhering to propriety and law. When Zhang was promoted to envoy and further enfeoffed as a Grand Lady, she lived out her days in the mansion in Luoyang. Alas, women and girls first suffer hardships before they encounter dangers and difficulties, experiencing them all. She first faced the peril of losing her virtue and seeking refuge, but ultimately enjoyed wealth, nobility, and a grand title. Reflecting on ancient virtuous men and gentlemen, when they were not yet fortunate, they often suffered under the dust and wore a look of distress, sometimes crying out to heaven for death but unable to do so. Once they established their achievements, they ascended like dragons, achieving high ranks and widespread favor, their deeds recorded in history and their names immortalized. How countless are such examples! Because of these writings, one can understand that women of low status should not be treated lightly, especially those with literary and martial talents who are constrained by their humble origins and low positions.

**Taizhou General Chongjin's Encounter with a Taoist** Taizhou General Chongjin began his career in the military, serving as a member of the vanguard under Emperor Taizu of Song. Through his accumulated hard work, he rose to the position of Commander of the Imperial Guards (both cavalry and infantry). During the reign of Emperor Taizong, he was transferred to command the military district of Yongxing Army. In his later years, Chongjin developed a strong interest in Taoism and firmly believed in the possibility of alchemy, specifically the transformation of base metals into gold or silver. There was a retired soldier named Hua Xiang, who dressed as a Taoist. The people called him Hua Xiang because of the intricate carvings on his neck. Later, he became a Taoist and introduced another Taoist to Chongjin, who became his fellow practitioner. Chongjin shared meals with them and provided all the money and silk they needed, without any disobedience. After a long time, however, there was no success in their alchemical endeavors. Hua Xiang then deceived Chongjin, saying that there was a person in the city of Jingzhou who was their master. He claimed that if Taizhou General could temporarily summon him, the alchemical process would be completed immediately. Chongjin sent a document to Jingzhou, ordering the temporary dispatch of the supposed master to Yongxing Army. However, Jingzhou refused to comply because there was no imperial decree. Chongjin then instructed someone to pretend to be ill and incurable, and the local officials reported to Chongjin, who arranged for the person to be removed from the military register. The supposed master from Jingzhou was eventually brought to Chongjin, and he was very pleased.

To obtain this person and go together to gather the missing medicine, it must be completed this year in August. It has already been six months. Before and after expenses, significant money and goods were spent, and there is fear that there will be no success in August, which will definitely lead to disaster. So secretly planned and devised a scheme to secretly escape. Hua Xiang has never drunk alcohol. [Pretend not to drink alcohol] One day, when it was dark, he finally returned to the official's courtyard. He was surprised by his late return. Upon arrival, he was already drunk. Angered by his late return, he confronted him, saying, "Respected teacher, you have always said that you don't understand drinking alcohol. Last night, you were heavily drunk, and your words and demeanor were stern." Hua Xiang smiled and replied slowly, "I have never actually drunk alcohol. Yesterday, I saw a fairy in the market. After speaking, he looked westward and bowed in reverence." Zhongjin said, "Who is the fairy, and where is he now?" Hua Xiang spoke with a serious expression and in a low voice, "It is Lu Dongbin." [At that time, everyone knew that Lu Dongbin was a god, so Hua Xiang said he saw him] Zhongjin said, "What did you say when you saw him?" Hua Xiang replied, "After seeing Lu Dongbin, he must invite him to drink in the market. I said I don't eat, and he said, 'But drink, and you won't get drunk.' I bowed and thanked him, and drank more than twenty cups. He still asked where I was, and I answered that I was here."

The Taifu Chuli said, "I have heard of this for a long time. The Taifu, a military person, can be so devoted to the Dao. This person has a long life but now already has sickness." [At that time, Tian was afflicted with a wind illness.] I will temporarily leave and bring a little medicine to treat it. Tian heard the words and was greatly delighted, saying, "Zhongjin, a rough person, how could there be a need for gods to descend?" And he said, "When will it come?" Huaxiang said, "This month on the fifteenth day at midnight will surely come." The Taifu said, "I do not wish to see many people. I hope the Taifu will arrange and set up the eastern position where arrows are shot, with new and fine mats in a quiet room, burning incense and lighting candles, with fresh fruits and good wine. The Taifu will purify himself, change into new clothes, prepare boots and a tablet, and wait for it at midnight; it will surely come down." Zhongjin said, "I humbly accept the instruction. By the appointed time, order the arrangement of the eastern position with curtains, beds, and mats, all new and clean, burn incense and light candles, purify, wear and hold, look up at the stars, bow and inform to wait for its arrival. Soon it was reported that it was midnight, but it did not come. Again take incense and burn it, bow to the sky again. At that time, Zhongjin was heavy-footed and also had a wind illness, making it very difficult to bow. That night, after bowing, he was panting, sweating, and his clothes were all wet, but he showed no sign of fatigue. Soon it was reported that it was the fourth watch, and Zhongjin was still burning incense."

The incense continued to burn, and the mind was filled with suspicion and astonishment as Heavy Advance stretched his neck to look forward, but there were no signs or omens. It was the fifth watch of the night when he questioned three people, including Flower Neck, and wanted to blame them for being false or exaggerated. A trusted person came to report that the master's door was wide open, there was no one inside, and the bags and chests had been moved away completely. It was because Flower Neck and others had deceived him into opening the eastern side gate, and they took the chests and all fled secretly. Heavy Advance was ashamed, resentful, and sighed, and just pointed and said to those around him, "bad men, bad men," and from then on, he no longer sought Daoist arts. At that time, a hidden person in Yongxing left two poems to mock him, placing the poems in front of the guard station. Tian ordered the guests to read them, and he was even more ashamed and then dispersed. He sent people to chase and capture them, but they were not caught. One of the poems was lost, and many people in Yongxing could recite them. I, as the Right Vice-Chancellor and Judge of Yongxing's military affairs, recorded this to warn greedy men. The anonymous poem read: Lead transforms into gold, and mercury into silver, Without reason, deceitful plans arise anew. One day, he was deceived by Heavy Advance, In the dead of night, he welcomed Lü Dongbin. The foolish man left the house, Leading his child, he had already fled. He only called out, "Three bad men," Laughing at the ten thousand people of Chang'an.

Encounter with Swordsmen in Wan Prefecture Bai Tinghai, the Grand Protector of Wan Prefecture, was the eldest son of Zhongling Wenke, who had retired from office. During his service as the Mansion and Estate Commissioner, he temporarily oversaw the five departments and the northern river inspection. After achieving merit in pacifying Shu, he was appointed as the governor of Wan Prefecture, was replaced, and returned, dying in Jingnan. Bai had a fondness for the extraordinary and respected the teachings of Daoism. His older brother, Tingrang, was the commander of personal guards but did not adhere to strict discipline and frequently wandered through the marketplaces. Suddenly, a guest asked Tingrang if he had heard of the swordsmen. Tingrang replied, "I have heard of them, but I have never seen them." The guest said, "They are in the inn of Tongli Fang, where they are called the recluse, the swordsmen. You can accompany me to see them." Tingrang followed his advice, and the next day, they went to the inn together, where they saw five or six people sitting on the ground in a circle. Among them was one with deep-set eyes, full brows, a dark complexion, and a yellow beard. When Tingrang arrived, only the man with the yellow beard did not stand up. The guest said, "You should bow." Tingrang bowed, and the man with the yellow beard received him in a seated posture, saying slowly, "Who is this person?" The guest replied, "He is the nephew of Lord Bai, who came with me to inquire about the recluse." The man with the yellow beard smiled and said, "You can sit and drink together." Soon, a wooden basin was brought, filled with several bottles of wine, and each person had a porcelain bowl placed in front of them.

A tray of mutton was placed beside them. One person began cutting the meat into large pieces with a cleaver, and using a ladle, they poured wine into bowls, setting a meat dish before each person. Tingrang looked at them with a difficult expression. The bearded man drank it all in one go, and the others did the same, reaching out to take meat and eat it. Tingrang looked at him with raised eyebrows and narrowed eyes, as if angry. Tingrang forced himself to drink half a bowl and chewed a little meat and that was it. After the banquet, they dispersed. Tingrang looked closely and saw they were all dog butchers and wrestlers. Tingrang and his fellow guests stayed behind to chat. One of the guests said to the bearded man, "Mr. Bai is a man of principle. Sir, please do not be so formal." The bearded man took a short sword from the bed, took it out of its scabbard, and played with it, then struck the sword with his finger, making a clear sound. Tingrang thought he was a swordswoman and got up to bow to him repeatedly, saying, "I am fortunate to meet a recluse; someday I hope to become your disciple." The bearded man said, "This sword has killed fifty or sixty people; they were all stingy and disrespectful, and their heads were cooked and eaten, tasting like pig or sheep heads." Tingrang heard this and felt as if he were covered in needles, filled with fear and dread, and he retreated. He went home and told his younger brother, Tinghui, about the incident. Tinghui, a nobleman, had always admired extraordinary and talented individuals and said, "How can I meet such a person? I can ask someone to arrange it." So he told his guests about it.

A guest said, "Just prepare the wine and food for him." The next day, between the hours of辰 (7-9 AM) and巳 (9-11 AM), the guest indeed came with him. White's brothers welcomed him, invited him in, and both bowed together. The man with yellow beard sat down and accepted them all. After eating and drinking, he said to White, "Do you have a good sword?" White replied, "Yes," and took out several swords and placed them in front of him. The man with yellow beard examined each one and said, "All are ordinary iron." Ting Rang said, "There are two swords in my room; please take a look at them." The man with yellow beard placed one on the ground and said, "This is also ordinary iron." He then took another one and said, "This one is good." He ordered it to be sharpened. The man with yellow beard ordered a pair of tongs to be brought and used to snap the sword. The blade did not break. He said, "Indeed, it is quite good," and threw it in the air as if it were a sword dance. After a while, he left. Ting Hui found him strange and kept him, ordering him to stay by the fence and treating him very well. The man with yellow beard usually spoke little and only answered "yes" or "no." One day, he borrowed a fine horse and went out for several days. He returned on foot after several days, saying, "The horse got startled and ran away, and I didn't know where it went." After ten days, someone returned the horse. Another month later, the man with yellow beard said to Ting Rang, "I borrowed ten pieces of silver, a leather box, a fine horse, and two servants from your brother. I will return the silver and the horse when I come back to Huayang." White pondered this, wanting not to give it to him because he heard that he was a murderer and a miser, but also considering the possibility of giving it to him.

The hesitant and indecisive one, Huang Xu, became angry and declared he would leave, and could not be persuaded to stay. Bai’s brothers bowed and apologized, saying, "Ten silver bars and one horse, temporarily borrowed for a small matter. However, the servants feared they might not be suitable for a reclusive scholar’s instructions and all followed his gestures and lent it to him." Huang Xu left without hesitation, mounting his horse and departing. Bai’s brothers could not understand why. Several days later, a servant arrived and reported that the reclusive scholar had arrived at the earth wall and was angry at the slow pace, and sent him back. Another ten days later, another servant arrived and reported that the reclusive scholar had arrived at Shanxian and was angry and sent him back. Bai’s brothers said that the swordswoman dared not secretly discuss, fearing being found out and suffering misfortune, and that she did not arrive for more than a year. There was a merchant riding the borrowed horse who passed by the door, and Bai’s attendants all recognized him. Bai heard and questioned him, saying, "I bought it in Huazhou for eighty thousand coins, with clear contracts, but the name of the seller has changed." Only then did he realize his deceit. Several years later, someone saw him in Shanxian, and it turned out he was always good at forging. Generally speaking, people have a superficial appearance but deep intentions, making it difficult to trust them lightly. Huang Xu pretended to be a swordswoman to deceive people, which is why Bai could be deceived. Those who write are also like casting a tripod to prepare for things, making it difficult for people to enter the mountains and forests without encountering anything or not being as good as that. He is also one of the most deceptive from ancient times. Gentlemen know, or like casting a tripod, warn, warn.

**Translation:** Records of Old News from Luoyang, Volume Three Imperially Approved Four Treasuries Records of Old News from Luoyang, Volume Four Compiled by Zhang Qixian of the Song Dynasty The Greatness of An Zhongling An Zhongling, whose courtesy name was Yanwei and who was from the mountainous regions, is recorded in the history of the Five Dynasties. He served as a high-ranking official in the imperial guard, but his name is lost. [See the reference.] He read several classical texts and had a rough understanding of their main ideas, browsing through historical records and biographies. He was sharp, eloquent, and wise, upright and cautious, and served An Zhongling for many years. As An Zhongling rose to prominence, he often relied on him. Wherever An Zhongling went, he exercised both awe and benevolence, and in matters of punishment and reward, he never acted out of personal favoritism. He always entrusted his advisors to deliberate on such matters before making a decision. An Zhongling was broad-minded and did not act out of anger or joy, regardless of the size of the matter. After consulting with his advisors, he would always summon D Liu at night to review the details. Therefore, An Zhongling, having served as a military governor of a major region and as the governor of the capital city, was highly respected and never known for being harsh or troublesome, largely due to the constant support of D Liu. An Zhongling, during his tenure as the military governor of Hedong, made numerous requests, some of which were even resubmitted, but all were suppressed and not replied to. One day, during a gathering of guests, someone mentioned this, and An Zhongling seemed dissatisfied, showing a slight displeasure. At the time, those in power remarked that all the matters he had requested were indeed feasible, and since he was the governor of the capital city and the military governor of Hedong, it was unlikely that all his petitions would be ignored.

There was some obstruction at your guest seat, and I hesitated and did not answer immediately. Mr. Liu whispered to the middle commander behind him. Mr. Liu said to the guest, "The lord's abdomen is slightly painful, and he is getting up." The guest agreed, and they all left. After the middle commander entered the residence, Mr. Liu followed him. The middle commander entered the inner gate and gradually reached the front of the hall and the central courtyard. Mr. Liu also followed him. The middle commander was surprised and looked back at him. Mr. Liu said, "I have something to say, and I do not want outsiders to hear it, so I came to the front of the hall and the central courtyard." I have served for a long time and have received much favor. Suddenly seeing what he has been doing recently, I am afraid of disaster. I cannot suddenly take leave and bid farewell. Today, I ask the lord to give me a charge and have me executed to repay the favors I have received. After finishing speaking, he held his handkerchief in both hands and threw it on the ground. With angry eyes, he stepped back, choked, and eventually fell to the ground. The middle commander supported him himself and had several female attendants help him sit down. After a while, he could speak, but he only said, "I am guilty of death." The middle commander did not understand, and the entire residence was filled with fear. The middle commander dismissed his concubines and comforted them with a lowered face, asking them the reason. Mr. Liu said, "The middle commander is already as noble as this, as wealthy as this, and as useful to the court as this. What more does he want?" Moreover, the lord's achievements, reputation, position, and prestige.

The court is not unaware that all the previous submissions were not meant to be carried out; they are actually good things. The middle commander said, "All the submissions made over ten times were not carried out; how can they be called good things?" Mr. Liu said, "If Lord Public has a fierce and dangerous disposition, this place is surrounded by mountains and rivers, the court must indulge him, and all matters, no matter how big or small, will be carried out; otherwise, disaster will quickly befall him. Today, the emperor is wise and saintly, and his advisors are capable; they understand that Lord Public is loyal and virtuous. The matters he submitted, though trivial and not carried out, are not doubted. Since the court does not doubt Lord Public, why should Lord Public doubt himself? In the morning, when facing the guards, I fear that Lord Public might say something inappropriate again, and if the chief ministers hear it, won't they doubt Lord Public? My family has only a few members, but Lord Public has over a hundred. I hope Lord Public will be cautious in his words. After the reply, I took earth and filled his mouth; Lord Public himself took the earth away." Mr. Liu said, "Ancient people who spat at their ruler did not care about their lives and sought a place to die to atone for their offense. How much more so, I am extremely foolish and disrespectful. I beg for a charge of treason and ask to be beheaded to atone for my disrespect to the ruler." The middle commander hastily said, "You worry about the master so much and yet say such things, turning me into an uncomfortable person. It's all because I am old and senile."

To make the translation more polished and natural in English, I will refine the language and ensure that it flows smoothly while maintaining fidelity to the original text. Here is the final translation: --- Because of your fault, you have caused such confusion, what have you done? I know my fault, and I will not do it again, so please stop, please stop. Call the attendant to take the bell and mirror, and the official took the headband from the ground, wiped it with the sleeve of his official robe, and had the maid wrap it. "D" shook his head and refused to accept it, so the official then wrapped the headband himself and had the maid arrange it. The official repeatedly comforted and apologized, causing "D" to cry and apologize, leading to several days of fasting until near death. "A" daily had his children check on "D" and treated him like family. Generally, people's nature is such that if they receive a drink or a meal, they will use sweet words to please them, and those who have eaten the food rarely know how to repay. If they happen to feel grateful, they will flatter and decorate their appearance to serve them. Observe "D," who was initially bound as a low-ranking official but later showed gratitude and a willingness to die for the lord. When he threw down his towel, he spoke boldly and clearly, making the lord realize his mistake and guiding him to the right path. The official wanted to place the lord in a state of no fault. Even for a minor mistake, "D" was willing to die to correct it, let alone for greater matters. ---

If we consider his character and conduct, even if faced with the threat of death or severe punishment, he would not be swayed. He is honest and straightforward, a true man of integrity who serves his superiors with a sincere heart. If he had served in the court and encountered a true ruler who recognized his talents, and if there were any shortcomings in the government or minor faults in the ruler, he would have boldly and frankly advised, even if it meant facing great danger, just as ancient sages did by pulling on the ruler's robe or cutting off the horse's reins. Moved by the kindness of the Middle Commander, he was reluctant to leave and wished to stay until his death, which is a pity because a person's virtue or lack thereof is not determined by their social status. Although Liu's position was low, his actions and conduct were very high. The Middle Commander then called his children and warned them, saying, "Do not think of this person as a fool. He spoke frankly to save me." There were many instances like this before and after, and he ensured that his entire family benefited from his good fortune and the generous treatment by the court, all thanks to this person. One day when I die, you should treat this person as I do today, ensuring he has no lack of anything. After the Middle Commander's death, his children followed his instructions, providing Liu with food, clothing, and other necessities every day, until his death. How virtuous they were! The Middle Commander should have been able to maintain his wealth and status, serving multiple dynasties with a good reputation throughout his life. From his words, we can see that he did not accept Liu's advice and remained arrogant and haughty.

Liang Zong, a virtuous prince of the Five Dynasties period, was known for his great tolerance and willingness to accept advice. He would sometimes act in a straightforward and unrestrained manner, throwing a towel on the ground and expressing his emotions openly. However, he would immediately respond with a pleasant expression, gentle voice, and kind words to comfort and please those around him. He would pick up the towel, wave it, and personally wrap and comb it, apologizing several times to show his humility. This behavior demonstrated his great tolerance for accepting advice and his ability to return to a better state. Liang Zong observed how a single word of advice was accepted as if it were urgently needed. By following this behavior, one could avoid major mistakes. He was a kind and respectful person who was good at interacting with others, and his filial piety and kindness came naturally. He treated his younger brothers and nephews with love and care. His younger brother, Liang, was fond of learning, and Liang Zong extended invitations to many scholars, providing them with generous clothing and food. As a result, there were often dozens of guests in the academy, and in spring and winter, the guests were provided with clothing, ensuring that none were left out. The guests were always full at his door, enjoying plenty of wine and meat. Liang Zong kept dozens of horses in Luoyang. Sometimes when he wanted to go out, the people around him said there were no horses in the stable. It was because the guests had not measured their departure and arrival, and each rode a horse out. Liang Zong became serious and said that it was not proper to call the scholars a group of "措大." If this happened again, he would beat them with a stick and wait for the scholars to return with a horse.

Upon arriving, he reported his benevolence and generosity, and also mentioned that he had collected a large number of books in the Western Study. Thus, "Shouliang" was a diligent scholar, skilled in writing letters and quick and adept at composing poetry and prose. He was first recommended by Kaifeng Prefecture and passed the imperial examination as the top scholar in one attempt, taking office as a military officer. Subsequently, he was appointed as the judicial officer of Fengxiang Prefecture. At that time, I had assisted in the compilation of historical works and served in the history bureau. I recommended him, and he was appointed as the deputy head of the Temple of Heaven. Later, he died in office as a high-ranking official. "Shouliang" was wise inside but reserved outside, filial, friendly, and trustworthy. Alas, Emperor Taizong gradually recognized his talents but did not fully utilize them before his death, which was also fate. "Shouchong" served in the imperial guard during the reign of Emperor Taizu, accompanied the emperor on a campaign to Hedong, built a dam to block the Fen River, and died of exhaustion under siege. When I was a commoner, "Shouliang" treated me well, knowing that his house was full of guests, and I was ashamed to mix with them, so I never visited. After he passed the imperial examination, at the beginning of his success, I sent a poem to congratulate him, and the gist of it is: "Once bowed to his face on horseback, wanted to visit but was unable to, from this, one can see that I never visited his home." Several years ago, when I visited his home, it was now owned by others. Reflecting on the past and present, I felt melancholic for a long time, concerned that the history books might omit this, so I write it down for future generations.

Song Grand Tutor Yan Jun observed Buddhism. Yan Jun, as recorded in official histories, rose from the ranks, skilled in the use of the spear. Initially attached to the army of Huazhou, he served under Zhuangzong, who established his rule. Yan Jun was appointed as the commander of the imperial guards for his military prowess. Following Kang Yanxiao, he led the vanguard in the campaign against Shu and was granted the position of Cishi of Yuzhou due to his military achievements. Yan Jun was strong, brave, and vigorous, able to run as fast as a galloping horse. As a junior officer, he desired to achieve remarkable feats and often faced enemies on the battlefield with a wide headband and two braids. For this reason, the soldiers called him Song Mang'er. Later, although he became a high-ranking general, people near and far still referred to him as Song Mang'er. In the early Zhou Dynasty, Li Jianyi Zhi Sun had a reputation for poetry and was called Li Luo. Yan Jun once asked Li, "Why do people call you Li Luo if you have the surname Li?" Li, who was stern and quick to anger, replied with a jest, "If the Grand Tutor has the surname Song and the entire court calls him Song Mang'er, what is the difference?" Upon hearing this, Yan Jun was very pleased and laughed with him before leaving. When he first conquered Shu, Yan Jun took possession of a general's house. The owner had died, but his wife was still alive, and he had many concubines and a fortune of tens of thousands. Yan Jun knew there were many hidden treasures in the house, and the widow kept it a secret, instructing the maidservants not to reveal it.

He then told the master's wife that he had no legitimate wife and took this woman as his wife, treating her with the same courtesy as if she were a wife. When he was appointed governor of Yuzhou, he traveled with her, and all the private property belonged to Yanjun, but he already had many concubines and grew tired of her. He planned to return to the capital from Yuzhou, killed her while drunk, buried her behind the governor's office in Yuzhou, and traveled by boat down the river from Xiangxi. In the evening, he saw a small boat with several women approaching his boat. He looked closely at them, and they were the wife he had killed in Yuzhou. She was dressed in bright, new clothes, hit him, and cursed him, saying, "You took my whole family, you took my money and valuables, after you took me as your wife, you dug up everything in my family's property, not leaving a single thing. I did nothing wrong to you, why did you kill me? I have already appealed to heaven, and I will get my life back." Her voice was very loud, and everyone on the boat heard her. Soon she disappeared. Yanjun was terrified and promised to donate to monks and perform good deeds. From then on, he saw her every night as before, until the night he arrived at Jingzhou, he did not see her. When he landed in Song, he first went to a temple, donated money to set up a feast, and performed good deeds.

Song confessed his sins and vowed to perform good deeds annually, with very earnest words. He confessed to the Buddha, and monks performed rituals, recited sutras, and burned the document. Sometimes, the document was seen once a month or every half year. Song would definitely perform the act of top-knotting and confess his sins when he arrived at the imperial court. He was appointed as the defense commissioner of Ruzhou and built a temple on the west side of the state. The temple's current title is "Dengci Temple," and it was specifically built for the deceased wife. Yan Yun served as a governor and general in several states, including Deng, Jin, Shan, and Hezhong, and retired with the title of Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince. He had a nature that was calm and could endure, and the female servants under his favor were severely beaten for minor offenses. Many of them still had facial tattoos. There were many people in his residence who recited scriptures and performed rituals, and he devoted himself to Buddhism. After witnessing the consequences of killing his wife, he understood the principle of cause and effect. He had a long career in regional administration and was both wealthy and long-lived. He died peacefully in his main bedroom, not without the effect of self-restraint and repentance. If he had not early seen the ghost of the wronged and always harbored fear, otherwise, the places where he governed would not have been free from unjust and cruel punishments. The appearance of the deceased wife serves as a warning. Some people say that his devotion to Buddhism was not his original intention. The history books have omitted this story, so it is recorded here in detail.

In the water, the reflection of the king's ceremonial attire was seen. During the reign of Emperor Taizong of Song, an elderly monk, tall and well-built, with a full figure and considerable wealth, was walking with a staff, leading an elderly servant who was white-haired and carrying a cloth bag. He ordered the old servant to knock on the temple gate. The monk opened the door and welcomed him inside. After ascending the hall, the monk greeted him and they conversed for a long time. The monk then ordered the old servant to bring some茯苓汤. The old servant responded and opened the cloth bag, taking out the茯苓汤 powder and two small gold bowls and a small gold flask. He heated the flask and poured the soup into the bowls, waiting for it to cool before serving it. The old servant was very respectful. The monk prepared a meal, but the monk said, "I have not eaten with this servant for ten days, so there is no need for food." He then rose and visited various halls, paying respects to the meritorious deeds. He noticed that the Buddha's hair-like marks were slightly smaller and said, "I have something better that can be offered to replace them." He ordered the old servant to open the cloth bag and take out the cotton, further untie it, and wrap each item in cotton. There were thousands of large and small beads, mixed with amber, agate, and large pearls, about a sheng in volume. The monk was very surprised. All the monks present came to see and admire the items. The monk selected a bead as large as the Buddha's forehead hair.

The Abbot and the Monks The Abbot and the monks expressed their gratitude repeatedly. The elderly servant took the items from the bag, and nothing else was said. The plainly dressed man bowed to the monk and then left, despite the monks' efforts to keep him. The Abbot and the monks looked at each other and sighed deeply, prostrating in reverence, and all called him an extraordinary person or a god. The Abbot sent a messenger to follow and observe him, but he was lost in the crowd at a busy place. The messenger returned to report to the Abbot, who became even more grateful. After ten days, the monk came back, and all the monks greeted him with utmost respect, far more than before. The plainly dressed man ordered the attendant to leave and said to the Abbot, "Previously, I observed the Abbot's divine appearance and bone structure. If he had not become a Buddhist disciple, he would have been a small king." The Abbot nodded in agreement and remained humble for a long time. The plainly dressed man smiled and said, "Do you want to see the master's form?" The monk replied, "I wish to see it." He ordered to fetch a large basin and placed it in the middle of the courtyard, filling it with water during the day. He sat for a long time. The plainly dressed man led the Abbot and the monk, first burning incense and bowing to the sky. Afterward, they looked repeatedly but could not see anything. If anyone knew, it would reveal a heavenly secret. In a moment, he had the monk extend his neck to look at the reflection in the water. No longer had the appearance of a monk, he met, wearing a flat-topped crown, a robe with hanging tassels, royal attire, and holding a jade tablet. The monk was surprised and delighted, bowing to the sky.

The cloth-dressed man ordered the monk to burn incense and look at the water where white smoke rose from the water, rising over a dozen feet high, gradually turning into five colors. When they looked closely, the water also appeared that way. After a mealtime, it finally dispersed. The monk invited the cloth-dressed man to ask him questions, but the man remained silent and retreated. Later, the head monk said, 'I regret being a monk, I dare not pay respects. The head monk, indeed, called him a god or a sage.' He also told the monk, 'Now that you have taken vows, you cannot return to your original clothes. You must be even more diligent. However, you should have a lot of money and silk, up to three or five ten-thousand strings of cash.' The monk became even more humble and fearful, saying, 'How could I possibly have such money and silk?' The cloth-dressed man laughed and said, 'Yes, in the market, there are several types of medicine. Just buy them according to the numbers and bring them here. I will prepare three or five hundred pills for the head monk. Each pill can be used to make a hundred taels of copper into gold.' The monk heard this and stood up, clasping his hands for a long time, then went down the steps to bow to the sky, and sat down again. He asked, 'How do you know the medicine is genuine?' The cloth-dressed man said, 'Just go and discuss it, and then bring it here. I will identify it myself.' The monk said, 'May I ask you to buy it for me?' The cloth-dressed man angrily said, 'Am I here to buy medicine for you, a monk?' The monk stood up, feeling ashamed and fearful, and apologized. He then went to the street every month.

The monk had been searching the market for a commoner for about ten days when he returned. Suddenly, an old man appeared in the market and asked the head of the temple, "Every day, I see him buying what kind of medicine." The monk replied, "I see him buying a certain type of medicine." The old man suggested, "Try going to the shop of a certain person on the north side of the small clear river inside the road and ask them if they have this medicine." The monk hurriedly went to visit the shop, but the shop owner was away for one or two days and would return. The monk, worried about the approaching deadline, forgot to eat and sleep for two days. He hurriedly went to the small clear river shop, which was already open. He asked if they had the medicine, and the shop owner took it out and showed it to the monk. The monk, who had never known this medicine, asked how much it would cost and what the price was. The shop owner replied, "If you want all of this medicine, it cannot be less than four hundred thousand." The monk was startled, and the shop owner, with hands in sleeves, blinked his eyes, silently ignoring the monk. The monk, not able to understand it, stood up and walked several streets, thinking over and over again. He realized that the amount of money was high, but if the medicine was made, three or several ten thousand taels of gold would be immediately available. He concluded that the medicine did not require a large amount and returned to the shop. He asked, "How much is needed?"

The shop owner who sells the medicine for money said that in the Daozheng Fang area of the capital, there is another shop owned by Zhang that also sells this medicine. Zhang requires 500 thousand coins to sell the medicine, but the seller's medicine will not be sold for less than 400 thousand coins, even if it is one coin less. The monk then went to Zhang's shop in the Daozheng Fang area to inquire. It turned out that the shop indeed has the medicine, and the price is not less than 500 thousand coins. The monk decided to return to the smaller Qinghua shop and buy the medicine at the agreed price. Once the price was set, the monk said to the shop owner, "Please bring the medicine with you and come to the secluded courtyard with me. We can show it to a friend and then pay immediately." The shop owner replied, "On the agreed day, I will come with the medicine." The monk agreed, and on the agreed day, he and the shop owner brought the medicine to the courtyard. At midday, a commoner arrived, examined the medicine, and said, "All of these are genuine medicines. One type is slightly inferior and can be exchanged for another." The shop owner said, "Except for the one type from Zhang's shop in the Daozheng Fang area, which can be exchanged for 80 thousand coins. I will send the monk to buy it, and the rest of the medicine will be left at the Qinghua shop. The shop owner will bring 320 thousand coins back. The monk used 80 thousand coins to go to Zhang's shop to buy the medicine, which requires 100 thousand coins. The monk bought it at the agreed price and returned.

Thus, he set up an altar and incense burner, prepared a clay furnace, fasted, and chose a day to combine and refine alchemical substances. The man dressed in plain clothes arrived at noon, and the old servant returned in the afternoon. The abbot sent a young monk to follow secretly. Sometimes he went out of the city gate, sometimes wandered in the market, and sometimes visited Longmen. He walked lightly and steadily. The young monks, seeing the man look back, would retreat, fearing suspicion. The furnace was lit, and it was said that it would take 360 days to complete. He taught how to add or reduce fire, instructed on cleanliness, and burned incense. Cats and dogs were all tied up. During minor storms, the monks chanted loudly, day and night without pause. The man said he would wait until the medicine was completed, then temporarily go to Wutai Mountain, waiting for him to return to open the furnace. After a year, the man did not return, and the monks burned incense and opened the medicine furnace, finding the cauldron and utensils unchanged, but the medicine had turned to ashes. They only clapped their fingers and sighed in amazement, fearing it was a divine warning not to reveal it. Later, some monks who left to visit another temple spoke with an old friend in Luoyang late at night, discussing how strange and unbelievable it was to make a profit in such a way. It was also a sign of extreme greed in monks. Those who know this story should take it as a deep warning.

The Ghost of a Wronged Person Seen by a Dyeing Worker in Luoyang In the early years of the Kai Bao era, in the Xianxiang neighborhood of Luoyang, there was a dyeing worker named Li who could make and decorate flowered clothes. People called him Li Zhaohua. He had a small family and was active, but he had a harsh and violent nature and did not believe in Buddhism. One day, a monk carrying a bowl came to his door. Li looked at the monk as if he were a wooden puppet. Although the monk stood still for a long time, Li did not respond with a single word. Someone who was similar to him said to him, "Since you do not repay the kindness, why not say something to make him leave?" Li replied, "If I say something, I fear he will come back again." Those who heard this laughed at him. Suddenly, one day, Li borrowed several scrolls of embroidered Buddha images and several volumes of scriptures, and offered food to dozens of monks. His neighbors were surprised. A wealthy man named Fan Cheng secretly questioned him. Li told him the truth and said, "During the time of famine in the late Western Jin dynasty, I was poor and had only a few strings of coins. I originally engaged in petty trading in the countryside. There was a man surnamed Sun who also had a few strings of coins. We traveled together in the countryside, going out early and returning late, always traveling together. Whenever we reached the outskirts where there were no people, the man surnamed Sun often looked around, sometimes ahead, sometimes behind, as if he wanted to move and spy on something. I realized this. One day, when it was dark and gloomy, I could not distinguish even at close range. I found a round stone and lost it. I suspected it. Someone secretly questioned me. I told him the truth."

From behind, he struck him, hitting his brain directly. He then hit him again with a stone on the head and face, causing his death. The body was discarded along the road, and all of Sun's possessions were taken and brought back. Ultimately, no one knew about it. Recently, because of the night market, I went out to buy cooked food. Suddenly, I saw a person surnamed Sun also buying goods. Fearing that he would report to the authorities, I tried to flee secretly, but Sun followed me step by step, making it impossible for me to escape. I then bowed and confessed to him, saying that I now have a little money and beg not to be reported to the authorities, offering some money according to the amount. I claimed that I had died temporarily and was now revived. Sun remained silent. I then invited him into a hotel and we sat together drinking wine several rounds. Sun slowly said, "Why did you beat me to death? After so many years, I couldn't find you." I confessed, saying that I indeed committed a mortal sin, feeling that you were going to harm me first, so I acted first. I then bowed and told Sun, "I have been searching for you for a long time but couldn't find you. It didn't help at all. I am not a human but a ghost. You killed me, and the villagers discarded my body in a half-mile distant dry well. Now it is filled, but there is still a slight well shape. My bones are still there, and I have no place to be reborn."

You bury them with new clothes so there is no regret. I will arrange flowers and make a promise to seek the reason for the loss and fast with monks to create small merit. This is for the wrathful ghost. Fan Cheng has always been devoted to Buddhism and understands cause and effect. He said to Li, "Why not quickly at the initial place of killing, search for a dry well, recover the body, make new clothes, recite scriptures, fast with monks, and choose a place for reburial? If not, you will eventually suffer disaster." Li was frightened upon hearing this and followed his advice. After several days of searching, he indeed found traces of a dry well. Upon digging, he recovered the discarded body. He made new clothes and bought land in the mountains to bury it. Several years later, Li and his in-law, surnamed Fu, quarreled and were seized and dragged to the South State Pavilion. The pavilion had an ancient prison with no doors. Li threw himself into the prison. Happened to have the chief pavilion clerk, surnamed Bian, who was just going somewhere else. Li then hanged himself in the prison with a belt on a tree. The pavilion clerk was thus punished. Alas, Li and his descendants are all insignificant people. They were killed at the end of the Jin dynasty. Until the early Kai Bao period of our dynasty, it was still seen. After the reburial, he finally hanged himself. Is this not clear evidence of ghostly retribution? I witnessed it in Luoyang. Therefore, I write this to serve as a warning.

**The Old Records of the Gentry of Luoyang, Volume Four** Officially Compiled in the Four Treasuries **The Old Records of the Gentry of Luoyang, Volume Five** Compiled by Zhang Qixian of the Song Dynasty **Bai Zhongling's Insight into People** Bai Zhongling, whose given name was Wenke, was from Hedong, Liaozhou. Through military service and accumulated merit, he rose to the rank of commander of the cavalry and infantry, and later became the governor of Liaozhou and Dazhou. While serving as the governor of Dazhou, he encountered Han Zong, who was appointed as the military governor of Hedong. Dazhou was under Han Zong's jurisdiction. Bai Zhongling's eldest son, named Tinghui, was then an internal commander. Tinghui often interfered with the administration of the prefecture. Han Zong heard of this and was angry that Bai Zhongling had not properly disciplined his son. He then reported this to the court, and Bai Zhongling was dismissed from his post. Bai Zhongling, on his way through Bingzhou, went to pay homage at Han Zong's office. Han Zong saw him and observed his dignified and composed demeanor. He questioned Bai Zhongling about current affairs, and Bai Zhongling's responses were well-organized and to the point, all hitting the mark. Han Zong was greatly pleased and held a banquet, inviting guests to enjoy themselves. At the time, Han Zong had already requested an appointment for someone as the deputy military governor of Hedong, but the request had not yet been approved. Han Zong then recommended Bai Zhongling for the position, and the court approved it. The imperial decree was issued, and Bai Zhongling began to meet with Han Zong daily, discussing the ambitions Han Zong had for the future, particularly his plans to sweep away the world in the face of the impending southern incursions of the Khitans.

The councilor's secret plans were the primary factor in achieving success, thus he was able to gain a rare and fortunate opportunity. After Emperor Gaozu of Han established his cause, he appointed the councilor as the military governor of Hezhong Prefecture. Upon ascending the throne, Emperor Gaozu further appointed the councilor as the military governor of Tianping Army. Soon after, the councilor was transferred to Shaan Prefecture. Under the jurisdiction of Qizhou, Puzhou, and Yongzhou, three states had rebelled in succession. The councilor was then appointed to lead a campaign against the rebels in Hezhong Prefecture, with dual responsibilities for both military and civil administration. During the reign of Emperor Taizu of Zhou, the councilor served as a high-ranking official responsible for military law and overseeing the campaigns in three directions. The councilor had long-standing friendly relations with Emperor Taizu of Zhou, who frequently summoned him to consult on military matters. After the suppression of the three rebellions, Emperor Taizu of Zhou honored the councilor by bringing him along on the campaign. At the time, the governor of the Western Capital, Wang Xiang, was corrupted by accepting large amounts of bribes, leading to widespread public outcry. Emperor Taizu of Zhou immediately issued an edict appointing the councilor as the temporary guardian of the palace, replacing Wang Xiang and returning him to his home. The councilor then secretly reported the matter to Emperor Hui of Han, who issued an edict appointing the councilor as the governor of the Western Capital, with additional responsibilities as the head of the central government. After several years of Emperor Taizu of Zhou's reign, the councilor requested to retire from office, humbly citing his old age. Emperor Taizu of Zhou strongly advised him, but the councilor declined, citing his advanced age.

Gong persistently requested, and he was finally granted the position of Grand Tutor and retired, allowing him to return to Luoyang for rest and recuperation. He was bestowed with a sedan chair and a staff for the elderly. The prime minister was ordered to prepare a farewell banquet at Ban Bridge to bid him farewell. All said that Gong followed in the footsteps of the Two Sues, and he was the only one in a thousand years. After taking command, Gong appointed Zan Xiang to reside in Run and serve as the chief military commander. Alongside Gong, Niu Congfu served as the training officer, often entrusted with important tasks. Gong believed that Zan Xiang had insight and strategy and secretly spoke to Zhou Zu. Later, Zan Xiang was gradually employed and rapidly attained a prominent position. The chancellor often presided over Kaifeng Prefecture. The title of king was bestowed posthumously on Shen Zhongling, whose courtesy name was Lun. Shen Zhongling often visited the residence of Bai Zhongling. When Zan Xiang became prominent, Bai Zhongling sent someone to secretly recommend Shen Xiang by name and surname. Zan Xiang then recommended Shen Xiang to Emperor Taizu. At that time, Emperor Taizu was in hiding, holding the military power of the world, and stayed under Shen Xiang's roof, forming a relationship as close as fish and water, as harmonious as clouds and dragons. Emperor Taizu often told the prime minister to earnestly request an official position for Shen Zhongling, but the prime minister refused due to lack of precedent. Emperor Taizu ascended.

The emperor summoned Shen, the chancellor, to meet him before he had been officially appointed, and bestowed upon him a red robe and an ivory tablet. For several days, people called him the "red robe recipient." At that time, the prime minister held the position, but still wanted to prevent him from being appointed to a non-official role. Why was this seen so late? When the Secretariat sought instructions, the emperor asked, "Should he be given what position?" Seeing the emperor's expression, the Secretariat hurriedly said, "Do you wish to appoint him as a capital official?" The emperor did not respond. The Secretariat then wanted to appoint him to a higher-ranking position, and the emperor asked, "What position should he be given?" The Secretariat looked at each other without speaking. The emperor then said, "Temporarily give him the position of Langzhong," and thus he was appointed as a Langzhong in the Ministry of Revenue. The prime minister still wanted to place him in a non-official position, and appointed him to oversee customs and markets in Yangzhou. In succession, he was appointed as a Cishi. When there was an affair in the western part of Shu, he was appointed as a transport commissioner, and supplies and food were quickly transported without interruption. He governed himself with integrity, and when the army returned from pacifying Shu, he was not tainted by wealth or lust. Only two people, the mid-level official and Cao Jiaying, were untainted. The founding emperor recognized the mid-level official's far-sighted strategy and deep understanding, as well as his calm and reserved demeanor, and greatly employed him. The rise of these two officials was also due to the mid-level official's ability to recognize talent. After the mid-level official had passed away, I was familiar with his family. As a commoner, I received his recognition, and detailed the matter, and recorded it.

Zhang, the Grand Censor, Known for His Integrity Zhang, the Grand Censor, was known for his integrity. He was over thirty years old and had never studied. One day, a fellow villager who was a scholar and a distant relative of Zhang visited him. The scholar asked, "Can I learn?" Zhang replied, "How can someone who is already an adult and not literate still go on to study?" Zhang was displeased and resolved to find a teacher to study with. Zhang lived about one to two li south of a main road, near a cluster of shops. One day, a scholar passed by and stopped to rest in one of the shops. Zhang bowed to greet him, and the scholar returned the bow. They sat down and talked, and Zhang was very respectful. The scholar asked, "Do you have a strong will and nature? If you are diligent and hardworking, you can study for ten years and achieve success." Zhang said, "If I study day and night without stopping, can I do it in five years?" The scholar was surprised and said, "If you have such determination, what do you have to worry about not succeeding?" The scholar encouraged him repeatedly. Zhang thanked him and requested to be his disciple. He invited the scholar back to his estate, prepared a meal for him, and kept him there for several days. Zhang wept and told his parents, "I have requested a five-year leave to follow this scholar to study." Although his parents were farmers and simple people, they heard their son's request and agreed. Zhang followed the scholar to study.

--- **Translation:** For five years, they went together without any news or trace, and the parents were worried about it. One day, he returned already in scholar's robes. Because day and night he worked hard and could understand both major and minor classics, all reaching the profound depths of the sage. He was especially skilled in writing letters with proper style and form. After several years, he became skilled in composing poetry and prose. The local people praised and respected him, and he was known far and wide. Thus, he became a well-rounded scholar. In his later years, he lived in绛台, and people from all walks of life visited him daily, his reputation was very high. In the administration of the绛州 governor, there was someone who privately accepted bribes from wealthy people, disrupting legal cases. The people of the county suffered from this, and the governor also knew about it but could not remove the corrupt official. Just then, an old friend of the governor, who was on a mission, passed through the county and also heard about it. The governor and the visitor secretly plotted against the corrupt official, and they were afraid that the court would find out. The visitor said, "It would be better to invite a righteous person to the banquet, and the affairs of the county will surely be rectified, and your subordinates will also reform." The governor repeatedly sought such a person but could not find one, and someone mentioned the name of the 大监. The governor said, "I have heard of him for a long time," and immediately sent a letter, clothes, horse, and saddle to invite him. The 大监 only replied with a letter of thanks, claiming to be busy with studies and citing a lung disease as an excuse for not taking up the position. The governor was surprised and said, "The poor scholar Zhang is so destitute, and I have already sent a letter, clothes, horse, and saddle to invite him."
